proposals.title: Industry-Specific SDG Materiality in Global Supply Chains: A Multi-Method Assessment for Contextual Sustainability Metrics
proposals.motivation: The uniform application of all 17 UN Sustainable Development Goals (SDGs) across industries for sustainability assessment is a prevalent but potentially flawed practice in corporate and policy reporting. This one-size-fits-all approach risks diluting strategic focus, misallocating resources, and generating non-comparable data, thereby undermining effective supply chain governance and green trade initiatives. A rigorous, evidence-based investigation into the differential materiality of SDG attributes is critical for developing actionable, industry-contextual sustainability frameworks.
proposals.hypothesis: The material relevance of specific SDG-based attributes for sustainability performance assessment varies significantly across industries, driven by distinct supply chain structures, resource dependencies, and environmental-social footprints. A core set of universally relevant SDGs (e.g., SDG 8, 12) will be identifiable, but their operational attributes and weights will differ.
proposals.method: The research employs a sequential multi-method design. First, a systematic content analysis of pre-2025 sustainability reports from three contrasting sectors (e.g., electronics manufacturing, agri-food, and mining) will code SDG mentions and linked actions. Second, an Analytical Hierarchy Process (AHP) survey will be administered to international supply chain and sustainability managers to weight SDG relevance per industry. Third, Emergy Evaluation (aligning with Sarkis's prior work) will be applied to selected global trade flows (e.g., China-ASEAN) to quantify the embodied environmental resource intensity linked to different SDG targets, providing a biophysical grounding for the managerial survey data.
proposals.contribution: This study will provide a validated, multi-tiered framework for prioritizing SDGs in industry-specific supply chain sustainability assessments, moving beyond generic checklists. It will contribute to the fields of sustainable logistics and global environmental accounting by offering a methodology to tailor SDG-based metrics, enhancing the precision of tools like blockchain-enabled resource tracking and Logistics 4.0 performance models.
proposals.novelty: While prior research advocates for SDG integration, this study uniquely combines managerial perception (AHP) with biophysical accounting (Emergy) to empirically test and quantify the hypothesis of non-uniform SDG materiality across industries, directly challenging the tacit assumption of universal applicability in prevailing assessment tools.

proposals.title: A Goal-Driven Technology Selection Framework for Sustainable Global Supply Chains: An Inverse Multi-Criteria Decision Analysis
proposals.motivation: Current supply chain technology assessments (e.g., for Logistics 4.0, blockchain) often start with the technology's features and then evaluate its sustainability impacts. This technology-push approach risks misalignment with urgent, science-based sustainability goals, such as net-zero emissions or circularity targets. A paradigm shift is needed to ensure technological investments directly and efficiently serve predefined environmental and social objectives within complex global trade networks.
proposals.hypothesis: A goal-driven, inverse assessment framework, which prioritizes sustainability targets as the primary selection criteria, will identify a significantly different and more effective portfolio of technologies for supply chain transformation than conventional, technology-first evaluation methods.
proposals.method: The research will employ an inverse Multi-Criteria Decision Analysis (MCDA) model. First, we will define a hierarchy of sustainability goals (e.g., carbon reduction, resource efficiency, social equity) derived from frameworks like the UN SDGs and the Science Based Targets initiative (SBTi), weighting them via an Analytic Hierarchy Process (AHP) survey of international supply chain experts. Second, a comprehensive database of existing and emerging supply chain technologies (e.g., IoT, AI, blockchain, electric vehicles, bio-based materials) will be constructed. Finally, a Technique for Order of Preference by Similarity to Ideal Solution (TOPSIS) algorithm will be used to rank technologies based on their projected performance against the weighted goal hierarchy, rather than evaluating goals based on a chosen technology.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide a novel, actionable decision-support tool for managers and policymakers to align technology adoption with sustainability imperatives. It will contribute to the literature on green supply chain management and sustainable logistics by formalizing a goal-driven assessment methodology, moving beyond descriptive case studies of single technologies.
proposals.novelty: While MCDA is used in sustainable supply chain management, its application is predominantly for comparing predefined technology options. This inverts the perspective by making sustainability goals the independent variables and technologies the dependent variables, creating a systematic, target-first selection mechanism.

proposals.title: Longitudinal Assessment of Sustainability Performance in Digital Supply Chains: A Multi-Method Panel Study of Logistics 4.0 Technologies
proposals.motivation: The rapid adoption of Logistics 4.0 technologies (e.g., IoT, blockchain, AI) is promoted for enhancing supply chain efficiency and transparency. However, pre-2026 research, including Dr. Sarkis's own work, lacks robust longitudinal evidence on whether these technologies deliver sustained environmental and social benefits or lead to unintended consequences like increased e-waste or energy consumption over time. This gap hinders evidence-based policy and investment in sustainable supply chain digitalization.
proposals.hypothesis: The implementation of integrated Logistics 4.0 technology suites leads to measurable, long-term improvements in environmental resource efficiency (e.g., reduced material and energy intensity) within global supply chains, but these gains may plateau or reverse without complementary organizational and circular economy practices.
proposals.method: We propose a 3-year panel study combining quantitative and qualitative methods. First, we will operationalize a validated Logistics 4.0 measurement model (from Sarkis's prior work) and pair it with emergy evaluation and Material Flow Analysis (MFA) to create a multi-metric sustainability dashboard. Second, we will recruit a stratified panel of 150 international firms from sectors like electronics and apparel, identified via global trade databases. Using structured equation modeling (SEM) and difference-in-differences (DiD) analysis on annual survey and secondary data, we will track changes in embodied environmental resources and social metrics (e.g., labor practices transparency via blockchain). Third, we will conduct nested case studies with a subset of firms to explore causal mechanisms.
proposals.contribution: This research will provide one of the first longitudinal, multi-method datasets linking digital technology adoption to long-term sustainability outcomes in global supply chains. It will contribute a validated framework for temporal sustainability impact assessment and offer practical insights for managers and policymakers on sustaining the 'green' benefits of digital transformation.
proposals.novelty: While prior work has assessed cross-sectional adoption or conceptual benefits, this study uniquely integrates emergy analysis and social metrics into a longitudinal panel design to empirically trace the dynamic sustainability impacts of specific technology bundles over a multi-year horizon, moving beyond snapshot evaluations.

proposals.title: A Policy Simulation Framework for National Sustainable Technology Adoption: Integrating Embodied Resource Accounting and Multi-Regional Input-Output Analysis
proposals.motivation: National industrial policies, such as those under the Belt and Road Initiative or post-COP26 climate pledges, urgently require robust, systems-level tools to evaluate and promote sustainable technology adoption across supply chains. Existing policy instruments often lack granular, embodied environmental resource accounting, leading to suboptimal outcomes and potential carbon leakage. This gap hinders the effective scaling of green industrial strategies from firm-level initiatives to coherent national or regional policy.
proposals.hypothesis: Integrating embodied environmental resource accounting (EERA) with multi-regional input-output (MRIO) models and policy simulation can identify high-leverage industrial sectors and technology pathways that maximize environmental benefits while minimizing economic disruption at a national scale.
proposals.method: The research will develop a simulation framework by first constructing a hybrid MRIO-EERA model for a target region (e.g., ASEAN-China trade bloc), embedding emergy and material flow data. We will then employ agent-based modeling (ABM) to simulate firm-level technology adoption decisions under different policy scenarios (taxes, subsidies, standards). Key algorithms include system dynamics for policy feedback loops and optimization routines (e.g., genetic algorithms) to identify Pareto-optimal policy mixes that balance environmental (e.g., CO2, water) and economic (e.g., value-added, employment) indicators.
proposals.contribution: This work will contribute a novel, scalable decision-support tool for policymakers to quantitatively assess the supply chain and trade implications of sustainable technology policies. It will advance the field of sustainable supply chain management by bridging firm-level environmental accounting with macroeconomic industrial policy, providing empirical evidence on the systemic effectiveness of different policy instruments.
proposals.novelty: The novelty lies in the integration of bottom-up, blockchain-auditable embodied resource data (from Sarkis's prior work) with top-down macroeconomic MRIO and ABM simulation, creating a dynamic, testbed for national industrial policy that is currently absent in the literature.

proposals.title: Inverse Sustainability Assessment: A Network-Based Framework to Identify Technological and Managerial Blockers in Global Supply Chains
proposals.motivation: Despite widespread adoption of green technologies and sustainability practices, global supply chains continue to struggle with meeting ambitious environmental targets. This suggests that certain entrenched technologies or operational practices may be creating systemic inertia, acting as hidden barriers to progress. An inverse analysis, focusing on identifying these blockers rather than promoting solutions, is a critical but under-explored approach to accelerating sustainable transformation in global trade and logistics.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that specific, widely adopted technologies and managerial practices in global supply chains create path dependencies and lock-in effects that systematically inhibit the adoption of superior sustainable alternatives and the achievement of broader sustainability goals.
proposals.method: The research will employ a mixed-methods, network-based approach. First, we will conduct a systematic literature review and expert Delphi survey to catalog dominant technologies/practices and sustainability performance metrics. Second, we will construct a multi-layer network model where nodes represent technologies/practices and links represent dependencies, complementarities, or conflicts. Using data from international industry surveys (extending methods from Logistics 4.0 research) and embodied environmental resource accounting, we will apply inverse optimization and constraint propagation algorithms to identify which nodes, when their adoption level is fixed as a constraint, most severely limit the achievable values of sustainability performance indicators across the network.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide a novel diagnostic framework for practitioners and policymakers to pinpoint specific intervention points for systemic change. It will contribute to supply chain environmental accounting by shifting focus from incremental footprint measurement to systemic barrier analysis, and to sustainable logistics management by offering a methodology to anticipate and mitigate technological lock-in.
proposals.novelty: While existing literature focuses on promoting 'green' solutions, this study inverts the problem to diagnose sustainability blockers, applying network theory and inverse optimization—common in engineering and physics—to the socio-technical systems of supply chain management. It integrates embodied resource accounting (e.g., emergy) with technology adoption models in a novel way.

proposals.title: Tracing the Discourse: A Historical NLP Analysis of Environmental Accountability in Global Supply Chains, 1950-2020
proposals.motivation: Contemporary corporate sustainability and supply chain environmental accounting are often presented as novel, post-1990s phenomena, leading to ahistorical policy and management frameworks. This overlooks cyclical patterns of environmental concern, such as those following the 1970s oil crises or 1980s toxic waste scandals, which shaped early corporate practices. A longitudinal, data-driven understanding of how environmental accountability has been framed, materialized, and forgotten in corporate discourse is crucial for developing resilient and historically-informed strategies for greening global trade and logistics.
proposals.hypothesis: Corporate discourse on environmental accountability in supply chains exhibits significant, measurable historical cycles, with specific intangible factors (e.g., resource security, regulatory fear, consumer pressure) driving its prominence, and that periods of intense historical scrutiny contain forgotten practices relevant to modern sustainability challenges.
proposals.method: We will apply a pipeline of NLP techniques to a large-scale historical corpus of news (e.g., NYT, FT), corporate annual reports, and trade journals (1950-2020). Using BERT-based models (e.g., pre-trained BERT, fine-tuned for historical text) for named entity recognition and relation extraction, we will identify entities (firms, industries, regulations) and environmental concepts. Topic modeling (BERTopic or Dynamic Topic Models) will trace thematic evolution, while a custom dictionary and sentiment analysis will score the salience and framing (risk vs. opportunity) of environmental accountability over time, correlating peaks with geopolitical and economic events.
proposals.contribution: This research will provide the first large-scale, longitudinal map of environmental accountability discourse in supply chains, empirically challenging its linear progress narrative. It will contribute to supply chain and sustainability theory by identifying the long-term cyclical drivers of corporate environmental action and offer practitioners historical analogies and forgotten strategies for current challenges in green logistics and global trade environmental accounting.
proposals.novelty: While NLP is used in contemporary sustainability research, its application to a century-scale historical corpus to deconstruct the evolution of supply chain environmental accountability is novel. The fusion of historical business history methods with state-of-the-art (pre-2026) text mining to test cyclicality hypotheses in management studies represents a significant methodological and conceptual advance.

proposals.title: Orchestrating Sustainable Supply Networks: A Structural and Emergy-Based Framework for Assigning Agency and Accountability
proposals.motivation: Global supply chains and ecosystems are critical for sustainability transitions, yet governance remains fragmented. Current approaches, like corporate social responsibility, often fail to address the distributed nature of environmental impacts and responsibilities across complex, multi-tier networks. There is an urgent need for a prescriptive method that moves beyond firm-level analysis to assign actionable sustainability roles and accountability within the network structure itself.
proposals.hypothesis: The agency and accountability for steering a supply network towards sustainability outcomes can be systematically assigned by integrating structural network position analysis with a resource-based assessment of environmental influence. Specific network orchestrators can be identified whose strategic position and resource control grant them the unique capability to enforce collective environmental goals.
proposals.method: The research will employ a two-stage, mixed-methods approach. First, we will use Social Network Analysis (SNA) algorithms (e.g., centrality measures, brokerage scores) to map the structural power and information control of actors within a case study network (e.g., a BRI-related trade corridor). Second, this structural map will be integrated with an emergy evaluation (as used in Sarkis's prior ASEAN trade work) to quantify the embodied environmental resource flows and dependencies. A multi-criteria decision analysis (MCDA) framework will then synthesize these structural and resource metrics to diagnose orchestration potential and prescribe accountability mechanisms.
proposals.contribution: This work will contribute a novel diagnostic and governance toolkit for sustainable supply chain management, enabling policymakers and lead firms to pinpoint leverage points within ecosystems. It advances the field by operationalizing the concept of network orchestration for sustainability, providing a method to transform diffuse responsibility into clear, role-based accountability grounded in empirical network and environmental data.
proposals.novelty: The novelty lies in the prescriptive integration of structural network theory with biophysical (emergy) accounting to assign sustainability agency, moving beyond descriptive stakeholder mapping. This creates a non-obvious, quantifiable link between a firm's position in a value chain and its assigned responsibility for collective environmental outcomes.

proposals.title: Networked Sustainability Accounting: A Multi-Agent System for Emergent Environmental Performance in Supply Chain Ecosystems
proposals.motivation: Pre-2026, corporate sustainability accounting and green supply chain management predominantly focus on the individual firm or dyadic relationships, failing to capture the emergent environmental impacts and resilience of the entire networked ecosystem. This firm-centric view is inadequate for complex, global systems like the Belt and Road Initiative or Logistics 4.0 networks, where sustainability is a collective outcome of interdependent actors. A shift in the unit of analysis is critical for accurate environmental resource accounting and effective policy.
proposals.hypothesis: The environmental performance and resource efficiency of a supply network are emergent properties of the ecosystem's structure and agent interactions, not merely the sum of individual firm performances. Network-level interventions will yield significantly different sustainability outcomes compared to optimizing individual firms.
proposals.method: We will develop and simulate a multi-agent system (MAS) model where agents (suppliers, manufacturers, logistics providers) operate within a network topology derived from real-world trade data (e.g., UN Comtrade). Each agent follows behavioral rules for resource use and green practice adoption, informed by pre-2026 survey data (e.g., from Logistics 4.0 studies). The system's emergent environmental footprint (using emergy or embodied resource accounting) will be analyzed using network metrics (centrality, clustering, resilience) and validated via case studies of China-ASEAN trade ecosystems. Simulation will employ agent-based modeling platforms like NetLogo.
proposals.contribution: This research will provide a novel framework for 'ecosystem-level' environmental accounting, moving beyond firm-level Life Cycle Assessment. It will offer supply chain managers and policymakers a tool to evaluate the systemic sustainability impacts of network design, technology adoption (e.g., blockchain for transparency), and regional trade policies like the Belt and Road Initiative.
proposals.novelty: It integrates multi-agent simulation with embodied environmental accounting (e.g., emergy) and network theory to model sustainability as a non-linear, emergent phenomenon of supply ecosystems, a significant departure from the deterministic, firm-centric models dominant pre-2026.

proposals.title: Mapping Production Succession Pathways: An Ecological Analogy for Resilient and Circular Supply Chain Transitions
proposals.motivation: Global supply chains face unprecedented pressure to transition from linear to circular, sustainable models, yet existing optimization frameworks often fail to capture the complex, long-term, and non-linear nature of this systemic change. This gap leads to fragile transitions and suboptimal investments in technologies and skills. Drawing on pre-2026 knowledge of ecological succession provides a novel lens to understand and guide this evolution, moving beyond static efficiency toward dynamic resilience.
proposals.hypothesis: That industrial production ecosystems undergoing sustainability transitions follow identifiable succession pathways analogous to ecological systems, where the sequence and interaction of transitional technologies, workforce competencies, and supply chain configurations are critical determinants of long-term resilience and circularity.
proposals.method: The research will employ a mixed-methods, multi-phase design. First, a systematic literature review will establish analogical mappings between ecological succession stages (e.g., pioneer, intermediate, climax communities) and production system states. Second, using data from international industry surveys (e.g., extending Logistics 4.0 frameworks) and embodied resource trade databases (e.g., EMERGY, MRIO tables), we will apply system dynamics modeling and agent-based simulation to map potential transition pathways. Key transitional 'species' (e.g., specific bridging digital technologies, modular production cells) and 'disturbance regimes' (e.g., policy shocks, resource scarcity) will be parameterized within the simulation environment.
proposals.contribution: This work will contribute a novel theoretical framework, 'Production Succession Theory,' to the sustainable supply chain management literature. It will provide practitioners with a diagnostic tool to identify their system's current 'successional stage' and a prescriptive roadmap for investing in critical transitional assets and skills, thereby enhancing strategic resilience planning for the circular economy.
proposals.novelty: While industrial ecology and resilience concepts exist, the direct, structured application of ecological succession models—with their emphasis on dynamic pathways, legacy effects, and transitional keystone elements—to the design and management of global production and logistics systems is a fundamentally new interdisciplinary synthesis.

proposals.title: A Risk-Embedded Resilience Evaluation Framework for High-Reward Sustainable Production Paradigms
proposals.motivation: Global supply chains face escalating systemic shocks from geopolitical tensions, climate disruptions, and resource scarcity, challenging traditional efficiency-focused models. Current sustainable production research often sidelines radical, high-potential alternatives like hyper-localized bio-factories or fully circular networks due to perceived technical and economic risks, potentially missing crucial pathways for long-term resilience. A formal methodology is needed to quantify whether the resilience payoff of these complex paradigms justifies their inherent risks.
proposals.hypothesis: Integrating dynamic, multi-tier supply chain risk metrics into a resilience evaluation framework will reveal that specific 'high-risk' production paradigms (e.g., decentralized manufacturing, closed-loop material flows) offer superior long-term resilience and future-proofing benefits compared to conventionally 'low-risk' incremental improvements.
proposals.method: We will develop a hybrid multi-criteria decision analysis (MCDA) model, integrating Agent-Based Modeling (ABM) to simulate disruption propagation in complex networks. The model will quantify risk exposure using pre-2026 indices for geopolitical fragility, material criticality, and network complexity. These metrics will be dynamically linked to resilience capacities (adaptation, recovery, transformation) through System Dynamics. The framework will be empirically tested via scenario analysis and expert validation using data from sectors like electronics or bio-manufacturing, leveraging insights from global trade and emergy evaluation studies.
proposals.contribution: This research contributes a novel decision-support model that guides strategic investment toward the most resilient sustainable production transformations, moving beyond risk-averse filtering. It advances supply chain environmental accounting by formally embedding dynamic risk-resilience trade-offs, offering a concrete tool for policymakers and managers navigating initiatives like the Belt and Road or Logistics 4.0 transitions.
proposals.novelty: The novelty lies in reframing supply chain risk metrics from a filter for rejecting options to a diagnostic tool for identifying high-reward resilience pathways, creating a formal synergy between risk analysis and strategic resilience investment for sustainable production.

proposals.title: Modeling Global Supply Chains as Constrained Planetary Subsystems: A Multi-Objective Optimization Framework for Hard Environmental Limits
proposals.motivation: Current sustainable operations and supply chain management (SOSCM) research often treats environmental goals as secondary objectives or soft constraints, failing to align with the hard, non-negotiable biophysical limits of the planet (e.g., remaining carbon budgets, critical material scarcity). This gap is critical as international trade and global logistics, central to modern operations, must be fundamentally redesigned to operate within these planetary boundaries, a challenge highlighted by initiatives like the Belt and Road and the need for robust environmental accounting.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that reframing supply chain network design and logistics planning as a constrained optimization problem with hard environmental caps as primary constraints will yield fundamentally different, more resilient, and biophysically viable strategic configurations than current cost- or efficiency-first models.
proposals.method: We will develop a multi-objective, multi-period mathematical programming model that integrates spatially explicit planetary boundaries (e.g., regional carbon budgets, watershed extraction limits) as hard constraints. The model will optimize for traditional economic and operational objectives (cost, service level) subject to these caps. We will employ epsilon-constraint and augmented Chebyshev goal programming algorithms to generate Pareto-optimal frontiers. The framework will be empirically tested and validated using a global multi-regional input-output (MRIO) database (e.g., EXIOBASE) and trade data, focusing on a case network such as China-ASEAN trade corridors.
proposals.contribution: This research will contribute a novel, optimization-first decision-support framework that rigorously quantifies the trade-offs between economic performance and compliance with absolute environmental limits. It will provide new performance metrics (e.g., planetary boundary utilization efficiency) and strategy formulations, advancing SOSCM theory by making environmental constraints a central, defining parameter rather than a peripheral concern.
proposals.novelty: The novelty lies in the explicit, non-negotiable integration of absolute, spatially heterogeneous planetary boundaries as hard constraints in core POM optimization models, moving beyond relative efficiency improvements to model operations as subsystems of a bounded global ecosystem.

proposals.title: Robust Multi-Criteria Decision Models for Global Supply Chain Transitions Under Deep Environmental and Ethical Ambiguity
proposals.motivation: Current sustainable POM models rely on precise, stable data for environmental impacts and fixed societal weights for sustainability criteria, which is unrealistic. The true lifecycle impacts of novel materials and technologies (e.g., for Logistics 4.0 or circular systems) are uncertain, and societal valuations of trade-offs between carbon, biodiversity, and equity are contested and evolving. This deep ambiguity can lead to decision paralysis or the adoption of supply chain strategies that are fragile to future scientific discoveries or value shifts.
proposals.hypothesis: Production and supply chain network designs that are robust to a wide range of plausible future environmental impact assessments and multi-criteria weightings will outperform 'optimal' designs based on single-point forecasts in long-term sustainability and economic viability.
proposals.method: We will develop a novel robust multi-objective optimization framework integrating pre-2026 methods: (1) using scenario-based robust optimization and info-gap decision theory to model deep uncertainty in impact coefficients (e.g., carbon, emergy) derived from trade and LCA databases; (2) employing polyhedral uncertainty sets to represent ambiguous stakeholder preferences across sustainability criteria; and (3) designing heuristic algorithms (e.g., evolutionary multi-objective optimizers) to generate and evaluate resilient network configurations for global production and circular logistics.
proposals.contribution: This research will provide POM decision-makers with practical models and heuristics to build supply chains that are sustainable across many possible futures, moving beyond single-point forecasting. It will advance the theoretical integration of deep uncertainty management into sustainable supply chain and logistics literature, particularly for transitions like Industry 4.0 and the Belt and Road Initiative.
proposals.novelty: While robust optimization exists in POM, its application simultaneously to ambiguous *technical impact parameters* (e.g., embodied emissions) and *normative value weights* for sustainability in global network design is novel, addressing a core, under-explored challenge in sustainable operations.

proposals.title: Mapping the Safe and Just Operating Space for Global Production: A Planetary Boundaries Framework for Supply Chain Research Agendas
proposals.motivation: Current production and supply chain research is fragmented, lacking a unifying framework to prioritize studies that address critical socio-environmental limits. With global trade and logistics driving transboundary environmental impacts and social inequities, there is an urgent need for a structured, science-based approach to align production research with the imperatives of staying within planetary and social boundaries, as mandated by initiatives like the EU Green Deal and corporate net-zero pledges.
proposals.hypothesis: A 'Production Research Boundaries' framework, analogous to the Earth System's Planetary Boundaries, can be developed to define quantifiable, interconnected thresholds for production systems; this framework will reveal that current research agendas are misaligned with these boundaries and must be actively reshaped by societal environmental shifts.
proposals.method: The research will employ a multi-method approach. First, a systematic literature review and expert Delphi study will identify critical production system thresholds (e.g., material footprint per capita, embedded carbon budget, decent work indices). Second, these thresholds will be operationalized into a quantifiable multi-criteria model, integrating pre-2026 methods like emergy evaluation and input-output analysis to assess trade flows. Third, using text mining and network analysis of major management and logistics journal publications (2015-2025), we will map the current research landscape against the defined boundaries to identify gaps and misalignments.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide the first integrated, threshold-based framework to structurally evaluate and guide production and supply chain research. It will offer a direct tool for policymakers, funding bodies, and journal editors to prioritize studies that keep production within a safe and just operating space, thereby bridging the gap between high-level sustainability science and ground-level supply chain management.
proposals.novelty: While the Planetary Boundaries framework is established in environmental science, its rigorous adaptation as a diagnostic and prescriptive tool for structuring an entire field of management research—production and supply chain studies—is novel and non-obvious, moving beyond mere metaphor to a quantifiable mapping methodology.

proposals.title: Integrating Material Flow Analysis and Life Cycle Assessment for a Circular Economy Performance Evaluation of Global Production Networks
proposals.motivation: Global production networks, such as those within the Belt and Road Initiative, face increasing pressure to decouple economic growth from environmental degradation. Pre-2026, Production and Operations Management (POM) sustainability studies often lack a systemic, physical accounting of resource loops and their associated multi-impact environmental burdens. This gap hinders the transition from linear to circular production models and the accurate environmental accounting of international trade.
proposals.hypothesis: The integrated application of Material Flow Analysis (MFA) and Life Cycle Assessment (LCA) to specific global production networks will reveal critical, previously obscured hotspots for circularity intervention and demonstrate that optimizing for material circularity can significantly reduce the overall life cycle environmental footprint of traded goods.
proposals.method: The research will employ a sequential, two-stage industrial ecology methodology. First, a dynamic, substance-level Material Flow Analysis (MFA) will be conducted for a selected critical material (e.g., copper, plastics) within a defined transnational production network, mapping stocks, flows, and recycling rates using tools like STAN. Second, the results of the MFA will directly inform the system boundaries and foreground data for a comparative attributional Life Cycle Assessment (LCA), executed using software such as OpenLCA with the Ecoinvent database, to evaluate global warming, water use, and resource depletion impacts of current linear versus potential circular scenarios.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide POM scholars and practitioners with a novel, integrated analytical framework for quantifying the circular economy performance of complex supply chains. It will contribute empirical evidence on the environmental efficacy of circular strategies in global trade, directly informing policy instruments like the EU's Carbon Border Adjustment Mechanism and corporate sustainability reporting.
proposals.novelty: While MFA and LCA are established in industrial ecology, their integrated, sequential application to model and evaluate the environmental performance of *global production networks* from a circular economy perspective is novel within the POM field, moving beyond firm-level or product-level assessments to a network-scale physical analysis.

proposals.title: Auditing Supply Chain Sustainability: A Triangulated Dynamic Network Analysis for Authenticity and Resilience
proposals.motivation: Pre-2026, global supply chains face intense pressure to demonstrate sustainability, yet greenwashing and strategic misrepresentation remain pervasive, undermining genuine environmental and social progress. Existing tools, like descriptive network mapping and self-reported data, are insufficient for verifying the authenticity of sustainability claims or assessing systemic resilience to shocks like climate disruptions or regulatory changes. This gap creates significant risks for policymakers and lead firms aiming to build genuinely sustainable and robust global trade networks.
proposals.hypothesis: A supply network's resilience and the authenticity of its sustainability performance can be diagnosed by dynamically modeling its structure with data-triangulated node attributes, revealing that networks with verified, decentralized sustainability practices are more resilient and less prone to systemic greenwashing than those reliant on influential but unverified central actors.
proposals.method: The research employs a three-phase mixed-methods design. First, we construct a multi-tier supply network using firm-level trade data and apply forensic data triangulation, cross-referencing corporate sustainability reports with independent environmental (e.g., satellite emissions data), social (e.g., NGO audit reports), and regulatory databases to create 'verified' vs. 'declared' sustainability scores. Second, we apply Social Network Analysis (SNA) algorithms (e.g., centrality, community detection) to characterize the network. Third, we develop an agent-based simulation model (using platforms like NetLogo) where nodes (firms) with these validated attributes interact under dynamic pressures (e.g., carbon tax shocks, supplier failure), allowing us to test the evolution of sustainability performance and network resilience.
proposals.contribution: This work contributes a novel diagnostic framework that moves supply chain sustainability from descriptive mapping to audited, stress-tested analysis. It provides policymakers and supply chain managers with a tool to identify true sustainability leaders versus mere influencers and to design interventions that fortify networks against systemic risks. The research advances the field of sustainable supply chain management by integrating forensic accounting principles with dynamic network simulation.
proposals.novelty: The novelty lies in the integration of forensic data triangulation for attribute validation with dynamic, agent-based network simulation specifically for sustainability auditing, creating a proactive tool to stress-test authenticity and resilience, which goes beyond the static, descriptive network analyses common in pre-2026 literature.

proposals.title: Modeling Sustainability Risk Propagation in Global Supply Networks: An Epidemiological Network Analysis Approach
proposals.motivation: Global supply chains are complex networks where sustainability risks, such as regulatory non-compliance, Scope 3 emissions, and unethical labor practices, propagate opaquely from one firm to another, undermining collective sustainability performance. Current firm-centric assessment tools fail to capture this systemic contagion, leading to ineffective, generic interventions. A network-level understanding is urgently needed to design targeted strategies that enhance the resilience and sustainability of the entire supply web.
proposals.hypothesis: Applying epidemiological network models (e.g., SIR/SIS models) and Social Network Analysis (SNA) metrics to supply chain structures can accurately identify critical 'superspreader' nodes and pathways for sustainability risk propagation. Targeted interventions on these high-influence nodes will be more effective at containing systemic risk than uniform, firm-level sustainability initiatives.
proposals.method: The research will construct multi-tier supply network maps using trade data and corporate disclosures (e.g., Bloomberg SPLC, FactSet Revere). It will adapt compartmental models from epidemiology (e.g., Susceptible-Infected-Recovered) to simulate the spread of a defined sustainability 'pathogen' (e.g., a specific non-compliance). SNA algorithms will calculate centrality metrics (degree, betweenness, eigenvector) to identify superspreader firms. Network contagion simulations will then test the efficacy of targeted interventions (e.g., audits, green capability-building) on these central nodes versus random or tier-1-only approaches.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide a novel, network-based methodology for diagnosing and mitigating systemic sustainability risks in supply chains. It will contribute to supply chain transparency literature by operationalizing contagion theory and offer practical, data-driven guidance for policymakers and multinational corporations on where to focus limited resources for maximum sustainability impact across the network.
proposals.novelty: While SNA is used in supply chain research, the explicit adaptation of epidemiological transmission models and the concept of 'superspreaders' to model the dynamic propagation of sustainability risks is a significant, non-obvious cross-disciplinary leap. It moves beyond static structural analysis to simulate dynamic risk contagion and test targeted containment.

proposals.title: A Game-Theoretic Analysis of Multi-Actor Dynamics in Global Supply Chain Transitions: Integrating Socio-Technical Regimes and Strategic Behavior
proposals.motivation: The transition to sustainable production within global supply chains is hindered by systemic lock-in and misaligned incentives among key actors, including buyers, incumbent suppliers, and green technology entrants. Pre-2026 literature in socio-technical transitions and sustainable supply chain management lacks formal, predictive models to analyze the strategic interactions and coalition formations that determine the success or failure of such systemic shifts. This gap limits the ability of policymakers and corporate strategists to design effective interventions.
proposals.hypothesis: Integrating Multi-Level Perspective (MLP) frameworks with formal game-theoretic models will reveal that the stability of unsustainable production regimes is a function of specific, modifiable payoff structures and network configurations, and that targeted policy instruments can reshape these structures to enable cooperative equilibria favoring a transition.
proposals.method: The research will develop an agent-based simulation model grounded in game theory, where agents represent supply chain actors (buyers, incumbents, entrants). Their interactions will be formalized using a combination of non-cooperative games (e.g., evolutionary games for technology adoption) and cooperative game theory (e.g., Shapley value analysis for coalition formation in green supplier networks). The model will be parameterized and validated using empirical data from international surveys (extending Logistics 4.0 methods) and embodied environmental resource trade data, simulating the impact of policies like carbon tariffs or blockchain-enabled certification.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide a novel, computationally grounded framework to diagnose strategic barriers and leverage points in sustainable supply chain transitions. It will contribute to both transition theory by formalizing multi-actor dynamics and to sustainable supply chain management by offering a predictive tool for evaluating the efficacy of different governance and policy mechanisms in aligning individual and collective goals.
proposals.novelty: While game theory and socio-technical transitions have been used separately, their integration into a unified, empirically grounded model of global supply chain dynamics is novel. This fusion moves beyond descriptive case studies to a formal analysis of strategic equilibria and coalition stability under different intervention scenarios.

proposals.title: Quantifying Control Points for Sustainability: A Metabolic Network Analysis of Global Production and Trade Policy
proposals.motivation: International governance for sustainable production remains fragmented and often inefficient, with unclear understanding of how policy interventions in one region propagate through global supply chains. Pre-2026, the rise of multi-regional sustainability standards, the Belt and Road Initiative, and carbon border adjustment mechanisms highlight the urgent need for a systems-level, quantitative framework to model these complex interactions. Such a framework is critical for designing policies that effectively scale sustainable outcomes without unintended negative consequences.
proposals.hypothesis: Global production and trade systems can be modeled as metabolic networks where specific policy nodes and trade corridors function as high-control-point enzymes, disproportionately influencing the overall 'flux' of environmental and economic value. We hypothesize that identifying these control points through network analysis will reveal non-intuitive leverage points for international sustainability governance.
proposals.method: We will construct a multi-layer network model integrating global multi-regional input-output (MRIO) data (e.g., Eora, EXIOBASE) with a layer of policy instruments (e.g., tariffs, standards, BRI investments). Adapting Flux Balance Analysis (FBA), we will define system objectives (e.g., minimizing aggregate carbon emissions, maximizing circular material flow) and constraints from trade and policy data. Metabolic Control Analysis (MCA) algorithms will then be applied to compute control coefficients, quantifying the sensitivity of system-wide sustainability outcomes to perturbations at specific nodes (e.g., a key port's green logistics policy, an EU regulation).
proposals.contribution: This research will provide a novel, quantitative methodology for assessing the systemic impact of trade and environmental policies, moving beyond case-study or single-indicator approaches. It will contribute to the fields of sustainable supply chain management and industrial ecology by offering a predictive tool for policymakers and multinational corporations to identify the most effective intervention points for greening global production networks.
proposals.novelty: While network analysis is used in supply chain research, the direct adaptation of rigorous systems biology frameworks (FBA, MCA) to model policy influence and material/value flux in international trade is novel. This approach treats regulatory instruments as integral, quantifiable components of the production network itself, not just external factors.

proposals.title: Mapping the Intangible Architecture of GPNs: An NLP-Network Analysis of Reputational and Policy Risk Propagation for Sustainable Supply Chains
proposals.motivation: Global production networks (GPNs) are increasingly shaped by intangible factors like environmental, social, and governance (ESG) controversies and trade policy sentiment, which are poorly captured in traditional economic data. Understanding how these non-material risks propagate is critical for managing sustainable supply chains, especially within complex initiatives like the Belt and Road, yet current methods lack the granular, dynamic firm-level data needed for empirical analysis.
proposals.hypothesis: Intangible reputational and regulatory signals, extracted from unstructured text, propagate through GPNs in predictable patterns and exert a measurable influence on the sustainability performance and resilience of connected firms.
proposals.method: We will employ NLP text mining on a corpus of global news (e.g., GDELT), corporate sustainability reports, and policy documents (2015-2025) using transformer-based models (e.g., BERT) fine-tuned for entity recognition and sentiment/controversy classification related to ESG and trade policy. The resulting firm-level, time-series datasets of intangible exposures will be integrated with global trade network data (e.g., Eora MRIO) to construct a multi-layer network. We will then use temporal exponential random graph models (TERGMs) to analyze the direction and strength of risk propagation.
proposals.contribution: This research will produce a novel, open-source methodology and dataset for quantifying intangible GPN flows, advancing the field of supply chain environmental accounting beyond physical resources. It will provide empirical evidence on how policy sentiment and reputational shocks cascade, offering managers and policymakers a tool for proactive sustainable supply chain design.
proposals.novelty: While prior work uses NLP for sustainability disclosure analysis or network models for physical trade, this study uniquely fuses the two to dynamically map the *non-material architecture* of GPNs, revealing propagation channels invisible to conventional input-output or blockchain-based accounting.

proposals.title: Policy Leakage and Environmental Performance: A Network Analysis of Trade Agreements and Sustainable Production in Global Value Chains
proposals.motivation: International trade and production networks are central to global sustainability challenges, as environmental impacts are often displaced across borders. Pre-2026, major policy initiatives like the EU's Carbon Border Adjustment Mechanism (CBAM) and regional trade agreements (e.g., USMCA, RCEP) are reshaping global production, but their net effect on global environmental outcomes remains poorly understood. This research addresses a critical gap in understanding how heterogeneous national policies interact within complex global value chains to shape aggregate sustainable production.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that the effectiveness of unilateral or regional environmental trade policies is systematically moderated by the structural position and density of the countries involved within the global production network, leading to unintended 'policy leakage' effects that can undermine global sustainability goals.
proposals.method: The study will employ a multi-regional input-output (MRIO) model (e.g., EXIOBASE, EORA) coupled with network analysis to map the structure of global value chains for key sectors. We will develop a panel regression model with network autocorrelation (using a spatial econometric framework with a trade-network weight matrix) to analyze how a country's environmental performance (e.g., embodied carbon, material footprint) is influenced by its own policies and the policies of its network neighbors. The analysis will integrate data on environmental provisions in trade agreements (from the OECD-EPIC database) and national environmental policy stringency indices.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide a novel, network-based framework for evaluating the global systemic impacts of trade and environmental policies, moving beyond country-level assessments. It will offer empirically grounded insights for policymakers on designing 'network-aware' trade agreements that mitigate leakage and genuinely improve the environmental footprint of global production.
proposals.novelty: While prior work examines policy impacts or network structures separately, this research uniquely integrates them by applying spatial econometric techniques to a trade-network topology to explicitly model policy spillovers and leakage, a methodological advance in global supply chain environmental accounting.

proposals.title: Axiomatic Stress-Testing in Sustainable Operations: Modeling the Contradiction Between Lean Efficiency and Climate Resilience
proposals.motivation: Established Production and Operations Management (POM) theory, built on axioms of efficiency, cost minimization, and stable environments, is increasingly strained by non-negotiable sustainability constraints like net-zero targets and mandatory supply chain due diligence. This creates high-stakes contradiction points, such as the inherent tension between lean, low-inventory systems and the need for resilience against climate-induced disruptions, which existing theory cannot adequately resolve. A systematic analysis of these foundational conflicts is urgently needed to guide both corporate strategy and theoretical evolution.
proposals.hypothesis: Core POM axioms related to waste minimization, global sourcing optimization, and continuous flow are fundamentally incompatible with strict biophysical and regulatory sustainability constraints, requiring new conditioned theoretical constructs that explicitly model trade-offs and breaking points.
proposals.method: We will first identify key axioms through a formal conceptual analysis of canonical POM literature. For each axiom (e.g., 'inventory is waste'), we will develop agent-based models (using NetLogo or a similar pre-2026 platform) where agents (suppliers, manufacturers) operate under traditional rules and are then subjected to stochastic climate shocks and carbon budget constraints. Comparative historical case studies of sectors like automotive (electrification transition) and electronics (conflict minerals compliance) will provide empirical grounding. The modeling will use Monte Carlo simulation and scenario analysis to pinpoint performance thresholds and failure modes.
proposals.contribution: This research will produce a mapped set of axiomatic contradiction points and a series of falsifiable propositions for a sustainability-robust POM theory. It will provide a concrete, computational framework for stress-testing operations strategies against future sustainability scenarios, moving the field from broad principles to testable theoretical adaptations.
proposals.novelty: While trade-offs are known, this work uniquely applies a formal axiomatic and stress-testing lens from computational social science to core POM theory, moving beyond descriptive case studies to model the precise conditions under which traditional theory breaks down.

proposals.title: Axiomatic Sustainability in Production Theory: A Relativistic Reformulation of POM Models Under Invariant Ecological Budgets
proposals.motivation: Current POM models treat sustainability constraints as externalities or boundary conditions, leading to suboptimal solutions within a fundamentally unbounded paradigm. The escalating climate crisis and binding international agreements (e.g., Paris Accord) necessitate treating strict carbon and material budgets as foundational, system-wide invariants. This shift mirrors the conceptual revolution in physics when the speed of light was established as an invariant, forcing a reformulation of mechanics.
proposals.hypothesis: Treating absolute environmental budgets (e.g., a fixed carbon cap for a supply network) as an axiomatic constraint, analogous to the invariant speed of light, will fundamentally alter the mathematical formulation and optimal solutions of core POM models, revealing non-linear trade-offs and redefining efficiency.
proposals.method: We will employ a two-stage analytical modeling approach. First, we will reformulate canonical models (EOQ, facility location, newsvendor) by embedding a strict, invariant resource/waste budget as a primary axiom, not a secondary constraint. Second, we will use system dynamics simulation and multi-objective optimization (e.g., NSGA-II) to analyze the emergent, non-linear behavior of these 'relativistic' models under varying ecological 'frames of reference' (e.g., regional vs. global budgets). Empirical validation will use pre-2026 trade and emergy data (e.g., from BRI/ASEAN studies) to calibrate model parameters.
proposals.contribution: This research will provide a novel theoretical framework for sustainable POM, moving beyond eco-efficiency to 'eco-constrained optimality.' It will yield specific, reformulated models that can guide policy and practice under hard ecological limits. The work will bridge conceptual insights from systems theory with the mathematical rigor of operations research.
proposals.novelty: The analogy to theoretical physics provides a powerful, non-obvious lens to reconceptualize sustainability not as a cost but as a foundational axiom. This differs from existing green POM literature by demanding a priori mathematical reformulation of core models, rather than post-hoc adaptation.

proposals.title: Automating Supply Chain Sustainability Governance: A Smart Contract Framework for Multi-Standard Compliance Verification
proposals.motivation: Global supply chains face increasing pressure to comply with diverse and complex sustainability standards (e.g., carbon accounting, conflict minerals, labor practices), but current manual and siloed verification processes are costly, slow, and prone to error. The rise of blockchain and Industry 4.0 technologies, as evidenced in Logistics 4.0 and embodied resource accounting research, presents a critical opportunity to automate and integrate compliance, a need acutely felt in initiatives like the Belt and Road where environmental accountability is paramount.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that a modular smart contract architecture, integrating oracles with IoT and certified external data, can automatically verify compliance against multiple sustainability standards with greater accuracy, transparency, and efficiency than current manual audit-based systems.
proposals.method: We will design and prototype a framework using the Ethereum blockchain. The core involves developing modular smart contracts, each encoding a specific sustainability standard's logic (e.g., GHG Protocol Scope 3). We will implement Chainlink oracles to feed verified real-world data (e.g., IoT sensor readings for emissions, API calls to certified material databases). A main orchestrator contract will aggregate results, trigger automated payments or penalties, and update a tamper-proof compliance ledger. The prototype will be tested via a simulation of a multi-tier electronics supply chain.
proposals.contribution: This research contributes a novel, actionable technical framework for automated sustainability governance, advancing the field of green supply chain management. It provides empirical evidence on the feasibility and design principles of compliance-oriented smart contracts, bridging the gap between conceptual blockchain-for-sustainability proposals and practical, multi-standard implementation.
proposals.novelty: While prior work explores blockchain for supply chain transparency, this proposal's novelty lies in its focus on encoding the *verification logic* of multiple, often conflicting, sustainability standards into executable code and designing an integrated system for their simultaneous, automated enforcement, moving beyond simple track-and-trace.

proposals.title: A Multi-Criteria Decision Analysis Framework for Sustainable Blockchain Platform Selection in Global Supply Chains
proposals.motivation: The adoption of blockchain for supply chain transparency and sustainability accounting is accelerating, as seen in initiatives like the Belt and Road and green trade proposals. However, firms face a complex decision with multiple, often conflicting, platforms (e.g., Hyperledger Fabric, Ethereum, VeChain) that vary in technical features and environmental footprints. A structured, holistic evaluation method is critically needed to align platform choice with both operational and corporate sustainability goals.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that a structured MCDA framework, integrating technical, economic, and environmental criteria, will yield significantly different and more sustainable platform rankings than selection based on technical features or popularity alone.
proposals.method: The study will develop a hybrid MCDA model. First, criteria and sub-criteria will be identified via literature review and expert interviews, structured into technical (e.g., scalability, interoperability), economic (cost), and environmental (energy consumption, e-waste) dimensions. The Analytic Hierarchy Process (AHP) will be used to determine criterion weights from stakeholder surveys. Subsequently, Technique for Order of Preference by Similarity to Ideal Solution (TOPSIS) will be applied to rank major blockchain platforms against the weighted criteria using performance data from technical benchmarks and lifecycle assessment studies.
proposals.contribution: This research will provide a validated, decision-support tool for managers selecting blockchain infrastructure, directly linking technology choice to sustainability performance. It will extend the literature on green supply chain management and Logistics 4.0 by operationalizing sustainability in digital technology adoption decisions.
proposals.novelty: While MCDA is used in sustainable supplier selection, its application to evaluate and rank blockchain platforms based on an integrated technical-environmental criteria set is novel, bridging the gap between emerging digital logistics and established environmental management paradigms.

proposals.title: An Integrated Emergy-Data Envelopment Analysis (EM-DEA) Framework for Joint Environmental-Economic Performance Measurement in Reverse and Closed-Loop Supply Chains
proposals.motivation: The transition to a circular economy necessitates robust performance measurement for Reverse Logistics (RL) and Closed-Loop Supply Chain Management (CLSCM). Pre-2026, existing metrics are often siloed, focusing either on economic efficiency (e.g., cost, ROI) or fragmented environmental indicators (e.g., carbon, waste), failing to capture the integrated, systemic sustainability of global material recovery networks. This gap hinders effective managerial decision-making and policy development for initiatives like the Belt and Road, where trade and environmental flows are deeply intertwined.
proposals.hypothesis: An integrated performance measurement framework that jointly models economic value and comprehensive environmental resource flows (using emergy) will reveal significant, non-obvious trade-offs and synergies in RL/CLSCM systems, providing a more accurate basis for sustainability optimization than single-dimension metrics.
proposals.method: The research will develop a two-stage hybrid methodology. First, a system-level emergy accounting model, building on Sarkis's prior work, will quantify all environmental resource inputs (direct and indirect) embodied in RL/CLSCM processes. Second, these emergy flows will be integrated with traditional economic data (cost, revenue) as distinct inputs and outputs in a Network Data Envelopment Analysis (NDEA) model. The NDEA architecture will specifically model the series structure of collection, reprocessing, and redistribution stages. The model will be empirically validated using secondary data on international electronics or automotive parts recovery networks, applying algorithms like the slacks-based measure (SBM) for efficiency scoring.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide practitioners and policymakers with a rigorous, standardized framework for holistic RL/CLSCM assessment. It will advance the field of supply chain environmental accounting by operationally merging biophysical economics (emergy) with operations research (DEA), moving beyond simple carbon accounting. The resulting metrics will enable benchmarking and identification of improvement levers across both ecological and financial dimensions.
proposals.novelty: While DEA and emergy have been used separately in sustainability assessments, their integration into a formal NDEA structure for multi-stage RL/CLSCM is novel. This approach uniquely captures the cumulative environmental resource intensity alongside economic performance at each stage of the recovery chain, a systemic view not offered by existing lifecycle or carbon-only methods.

proposals.title: A Multi-Agent System Model for Global Circular Value Chains: Integrating Cross-Border Regulations, Logistics, and Emergy Accounting
proposals.motivation: The transition from linear to circular supply chains is critical for global sustainability, yet scaling Reverse Logistics and Closed-Loop Supply Chains (RL&CLSCM) internationally faces severe complexities. Pre-2026, these include fragmented cross-border environmental regulations, prohibitive logistics costs for returned/remanufactured goods, and a lack of integrated models to account for global resource flows and their true environmental impact. The Belt and Road Initiative and other mega-trade frameworks amplify these challenges, creating an urgent need for a holistic analytical framework.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that a multi-agent system (MAS) model, integrating emergy evaluation for environmental resource accounting and blockchain-based data layers for regulatory compliance, can effectively simulate and optimize the trade-offs between economic viability, logistical efficiency, and environmental performance in global circular value chains.
proposals.method: The research will develop a computational simulation using a multi-agent system architecture, where agents represent firms, logistics providers, and regulatory bodies across different jurisdictions. The model will incorporate: (1) an emergy evaluation module, building on Sarkis's prior work, to quantify embodied environmental resources in cross-border material flows; (2) a logistics cost and carbon footprint module informed by Logistics 4.0 survey data; and (3) a rule-based engine to simulate heterogeneous international regulations (e.g., EU Waste Shipment Regulation, Basel Convention). Optimization algorithms, such as agent-based heuristic search, will be used to identify configurations that minimize system-wide emergy intensity and cost while adhering to regulatory constraints.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide a novel, integrated simulation tool for policymakers and multinational corporations to assess the systemic implications of circular economy strategies at a global scale. It will advance the field of global supply chain environmental accounting by dynamically linking macro-level emergy flows with micro-level operational and regulatory decisions, offering empirical insights into the scalability of circular business models.
proposals.novelty: While prior work has examined RL/CLSCM or emergy accounting in isolation, this proposal uniquely integrates them within a dynamic, agent-based simulation of global trade, explicitly modeling the friction caused by disparate national regulations—a non-obvious synthesis of Sarkis's recent themes on blockchain, emergy, Logistics 4.0, and Belt and Road trade.

proposals.title: A Forward-Only Waste Accounting Framework for Global Supply Chains: Modeling and Minimizing Pre-Recovery Residuals
proposals.motivation: Sustainable supply chain management has focused heavily on reverse logistics and circular economy recovery, but this downstream focus can obscure the fundamental need to prevent waste generation upstream. In the pre-2026 context of escalating global trade and complex multi-tier networks, a systematic method to quantify and minimize waste as an inherent output of forward supply chain processes is critically lacking. This gap hinders the development of truly preventative environmental strategies aligned with global sustainability goals.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that modeling a supply chain as a forward-only, waste-generating system using embodied environmental resource accounting will reveal specific, actionable leverage points for waste minimization that are overlooked by recovery-focused models. The inverse relationship between forward waste generation and potential recovery efficiency can be quantitatively established.
proposals.method: The research will employ a hybrid method integrating Emergy Analysis (as used in Sarkis's prior BRI work) with Input-Output (IO) modeling of global trade networks. A multi-layer analytical framework will be developed: first, mapping a product's supply chain to identify material and energy transformation nodes; second, applying emergy algebra to allocate environmental resource inputs (solar emjoules) to each node's useful output and waste residuals; third, using Data Envelopment Analysis (DEA) to benchmark the waste-generation efficiency of different chain configurations. The model will be empirically tested using OECD-ASEAN trade data and corporate case studies.
proposals.contribution: This work will contribute a novel accounting framework that shifts the analytical boundary to the point of waste generation, providing a diagnostic tool for 'green trade' policy and corporate supply chain design. It will advance the field of supply chain environmental accounting by formally treating waste not as an externality for later management, but as a key, minimizable performance metric of the forward chain.
proposals.novelty: The novelty lies in explicitly inverting the recovery paradigm to create a forward-only waste minimization model, and in the integration of emergy evaluation with network efficiency analysis specifically for pre-recovery waste diagnostics in global supply chains—a combination not found in pre-2026 literature.

proposals.title: A Practitioner Decision-Support System for Sustainable Supplier Selection: Integrating Grey-BWM-TODIM in an Open-Source Software Tool
proposals.motivation: Sustainable supply chain management requires practitioners to evaluate suppliers against complex, often uncertain, economic, environmental, and social criteria. While advanced multi-criteria decision-making (MCDM) methods like the integrated Grey-BWM-TODIM exist in the literature, they remain largely inaccessible to managers due to their computational complexity and lack of user-friendly implementation. This gap hinders the practical adoption of robust sustainability assessment tools in global trade and logistics operations.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that implementing the integrated Grey-BWM-TODIM method within a dedicated, open-source software tool will significantly increase its practical usability and adoption by supply chain practitioners, leading to more consistent and transparent sustainable supplier evaluations.
proposals.method: The research will develop a standalone desktop application using the Python programming language and the PyQt framework for the graphical user interface (GUI). The core algorithm will implement the Grey Best-Worst Method (G-BWM) to determine criterion weights under uncertainty using grey system theory, followed by the TODIM (an acronym in Portuguese for Interactive and Multi-criteria Decision Making) method, which models decision-maker psychology via a prospect theory-based gain/loss function, to rank suppliers. The software architecture will include modules for data input (crisp, interval, linguistic), calculation engine, sensitivity analysis, and exportable results reporting.
proposals.contribution: This work contributes a validated, open-source software tool that operationalizes a sophisticated MCDM framework for sustainable supply chain decisions, directly bridging the academia-practice gap. It advances the field of sustainable logistics by providing a tangible mechanism for implementing environmental and social accounting in supplier selection, as called for in global supply chain research.
proposals.novelty: While the Grey-BWM-TODIM integration has been proposed theoretically, its implementation as a dedicated, freely available decision-support system (DSS) for supply chain managers is novel. This moves beyond conceptual models and empirical surveys to deliver a functional tool tailored for practitioner use in complex, real-world trade environments.

proposals.title: Mapping the Shadow Chain: A Multi-Tier, Multi-Method Framework for Assessing Social Risks in Sub-Tier Supplier Networks
proposals.motivation: Pre-2026, corporate sustainability due diligence is expanding beyond direct suppliers, driven by regulations like the EU's proposed Corporate Sustainability Due Diligence Directive. However, assessing social risks (e.g., forced labor, unsafe working conditions) in sub-tier (Tier-2, Tier-3+) suppliers remains a critical blind spot due to data opacity and complex network interdependencies. This gap exposes firms to significant reputational, legal, and operational risks, undermining global efforts for ethical supply chains.
proposals.hypothesis: Integrating multi-source data (trade, firm-level, and contextual data) within a network analysis and multi-criteria decision-making (MCDM) framework can effectively identify and prioritize high-social-risk hotspots in sub-tier supply networks, which are not apparent from tier-1 assessments alone.
proposals.method: The research will employ a three-phase, mixed-methods approach. First, we will construct a multi-tier supply network model using global trade databases (e.g., Eora MRIO) and firm-level data, applying network propagation algorithms to infer sub-tier connections. Second, we will develop a social risk index using a Fuzzy Analytical Hierarchy Process (FAHP) to weight and integrate heterogeneous indicators (e.g., regional governance scores, sector-specific risk data, news sentiment analysis). Third, the framework will be empirically validated through a focused case study on a critical mineral (e.g., cobalt) supply chain linking to ASEAN or Belt and Road Initiative partners, using survey and interview data from tier-1 suppliers to ground-truth sub-tier mappings.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide a scalable, methodological framework for sub-tier social risk assessment, directly addressing a core limitation in sustainable supply chain management. It will contribute to theory by integrating network science with social sustainability indicators, and offer practitioners a actionable tool for targeted due diligence and engagement deep in the supply chain.
proposals.novelty: While prior work focuses on environmental flows or direct supplier evaluation, this proposal uniquely scales down social risk assessment by combining embodied trade network analysis with a fuzzy MCDM model tailored for the uncertainty inherent in sub-tier data, moving beyond single-tier or single-method approaches.

proposals.title: A Multi-Criteria Portfolio Optimization Model for Sustainable Supplier Management: Integrating Social Performance, Cost, and Risk
proposals.motivation: Global supply chains face increasing pressure to manage portfolios of suppliers not just for cost and operational risk, but also for social sustainability performance (e.g., labor practices, community impact). Pre-2026, existing supplier selection models often treat these dimensions in isolation or fail to provide scalable portfolio-level optimization for diverse procurement categories, creating a critical gap for firms implementing the UN Sustainable Development Goals across complex, multi-tier networks.
proposals.hypothesis: A multi-criteria portfolio optimization model that explicitly integrates quantitative social performance indicators with traditional cost and risk metrics can generate supplier allocation strategies that significantly improve overall supply chain sustainability without disproportionate cost increases.
proposals.method: We will develop a multi-objective optimization model using a modified Markowitz portfolio theory framework, where 'return' is a composite sustainability-social score and 'risk' encompasses operational, financial, and reputational uncertainties. Supplier data (cost, audit scores, geographic risk indices) will be structured via a hierarchical Analytical Network Process (ANP) to weight criteria. The non-dominated sorting genetic algorithm II (NSGA-II) will be employed to solve the large-scale, multi-category optimization problem and generate the Pareto-optimal frontier of supplier portfolios.
proposals.contribution: This research will provide a novel, scalable decision-support framework for holistic supplier portfolio management, advancing the field of sustainable supply chain operations. It will offer empirically testable insights into the trade-offs and synergies between social performance, cost, and risk in global procurement.
proposals.novelty: The approach moves beyond single-supplier selection or dyadic evaluation by applying financial portfolio optimization logic to the multi-category supplier management problem, with a unique integration of quantifiable social performance metrics as a core objective rather than a constraint.

proposals.title: Inverting the Principal-Agent Lens: A Knowledge-Based Framework for Aligning Incentives in Circular Supply Chains
proposals.motivation: The transition to circular supply chains (CSCs) is hindered by misaligned incentives between actors, such as manufacturers (principals) and specialized recyclers or remanufacturers (agents). Traditional agency theory assumes the principal has superior information, but in CSCs, the agent often possesses critical, asymmetric knowledge about material flows, recovery technologies, and secondary market value. This inversion creates unique governance failures that impede circular performance.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that supply chain contracts which formally recognize and incentivize the agent's superior circular knowledge will lead to significantly higher levels of material recovery, economic value retention, and environmental performance compared to traditional principal-agent models.
proposals.method: The research will employ a mixed-methods design. First, a qualitative multiple-case study of 8-10 manufacturer-recycler dyads will map knowledge asymmetries and existing contracting practices. Second, building on pre-2026 multi-objective optimization and game theory literature, we will develop an agent-based simulation (using NetLogo or AnyLogic) to model incentive structures. The model will incorporate algorithms for knowledge-sharing rewards and penalty functions based on emergy accounting (reflecting Sarkis's prior work) to evaluate environmental-economic trade-offs under different contract scenarios.
proposals.contribution: This work will contribute a novel theoretical extension of agency theory for the circular economy context. Practically, it will provide a framework for designing 'knowledge-sensitive' contracts and governance mechanisms, directly supporting the operational goals of Logistics 4.0 and global green trade initiatives like the Belt and Road.
proposals.novelty: The novelty lies in systematically inverting the core assumption of agency theory to address circular supply chains, and in integrating knowledge governance with quantitative environmental accounting (e.g., emergy, blockchain-enabled tracking) to create verifiable, aligned incentives.

proposals.title: Modeling the Complexity of Global Circular Supply Networks: A Multi-Method Integration of Emergy, Blockchain, and Agent-Based Simulation
proposals.motivation: The transition from linear to circular supply chains is critical for global sustainability, but scaling circularity principles to complex, multi-tier global networks remains a significant challenge. Pre-2026, most models are either conceptual or limited to single-firm or regional analyses, failing to capture the dynamic interdependencies, material flows, and information asymmetries inherent in global circular networks. This gap hinders effective policy and managerial decision-making for initiatives like the Belt and Road, where trade and environmental impacts are deeply intertwined.
proposals.hypothesis: The systemic complexity and environmental performance of global circular supply networks can be effectively modeled and understood by integrating embodied environmental accounting, distributed ledger technology for traceability, and simulation of adaptive agent behaviors. We hypothesize that this integrated model will reveal non-linear trade-offs between circularity efficiency, economic viability, and environmental resource preservation at a global scale.
