proposals.method: We propose a three-phase, multi-method approach. First, we will employ Emergy Analysis, as used in Sarkis's prior work, to quantify the embodied environmental resource flows (e.g., from ASEAN trade) within a defined global network. Second, we will design a conceptual blockchain architecture, extending prior designs, to model transparent tracking of material provenance and circularity credits across tiers. Third, these elements will be integrated into an Agent-Based Model (ABM) developed in NetLogo or AnyLogic, where firms (agents) at different tiers make decisions based on cost, embodied emergy signals, and blockchain-verified information, allowing us to simulate network resilience and circularity outcomes under different policy and market scenarios.
proposals.contribution: This research will contribute a novel, integrated modeling framework for analyzing global circular supply networks, moving beyond static, firm-level assessments. It will provide empirically grounded insights into the systemic barriers and enablers of circularity, offering policymakers and multinational corporations a decision-support tool for evaluating trade and environmental strategies. The work will advance the theoretical understanding of how information transparency (via blockchain) and biophysical accounting (via emergy) interact within complex adaptive supply systems.
proposals.novelty: The novelty lies in the explicit integration of Emergy Analysis (a biophysical sustainability metric) with blockchain-enabled traceability architectures within an Agent-Based Modeling simulation to capture the dynamic, multi-tier complexity of global circular networks—a synthesis not found in pre-2026 literature. This moves the field from descriptive frameworks and isolated technology studies to a testable, computational system-dynamics model.

proposals.title: System Dynamics Modeling of the Lean-Green Paradox: Feedback Loops, Trade-offs, and Synergies in Global Supply Chains
proposals.motivation: The simultaneous pursuit of lean (efficiency) and green (sustainability) goals in supply chains is a critical but complex managerial challenge. Pre-2026 literature often treats these as independent or presents static trade-offs, failing to capture their dynamic, time-dependent interactions, which can lead to unintended consequences like the 'lean-green paradox' where efficiency gains inadvertently increase environmental impact. Understanding these feedback mechanisms is essential for designing resilient, sustainable global logistics systems, especially within initiatives like the Belt and Road.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that the relationship between lean efficiency gains and environmental impact is governed by reinforcing and balancing feedback loops, where short-term synergies can shift to long-term trade-offs (or vice-versa) depending on system structure, policy interventions, and market feedback.
proposals.method: We will develop a system dynamics (SD) simulation model using a stock-and-flow architecture (e.g., in Stella or Vensim) to map causal loops between key variables: inventory levels, transportation frequency, production efficiency, resource consumption, emissions, and regulatory/customer pressure. The model will be parameterized using empirical data from international surveys (extending Logistics 4.0 methods) and embodied environmental resource accounting (e.g., emergy, as in prior BRI work). Simulation scenarios will test the impact of policies like carbon pricing or blockchain-enabled transparency on the lean-green dynamic over a 10-20 year horizon.
proposals.contribution: This research will provide a dynamic, simulation-based framework to visualize and quantify the long-term interplay between operational efficiency and environmental performance, moving beyond static correlations. It will offer managers and policymakers a decision-support tool to anticipate unintended consequences and identify leverage points for synergistic lean-green strategies within complex global supply networks.
proposals.novelty: While lean and green have been studied separately, applying system dynamics to model their co-evolutionary feedback loops in a global supply chain context is novel. It integrates Sarkis's expertise in embodied resource accounting (e.g., emergy, blockchain for environmental accounting) with dynamic simulation to address temporal complexities previously overlooked.

proposals.title: Inverse Design of Supply Chain Interventions: A Multi-Method Framework for Deriving Lean-Green Practice Portfolios from Target Sustainability Outcomes
proposals.motivation: Current supply chain sustainability research predominantly adopts a forward-looking approach, modeling how specific practices lead to uncertain outcomes. This leaves practitioners with a critical gap: given a firm's specific, mandated sustainability targets (e.g., Science-Based Targets, circular economy goals), what precise combination of lean and green operational practices is required to achieve them? Addressing this inverse problem is essential for evidence-based, actionable decarbonization and resource-efficiency strategies in global trade networks.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that for a given set of target sustainability outcomes (environmental and operational), there exists a non-linear, context-dependent portfolio of lean and green supply chain practices that can be systematically identified and validated.
proposals.method: We will develop a two-stage, multi-method analytical framework. First, we will employ an inverse optimization algorithm, building on pre-2026 work in inverse linear programming, to solve for practice configurations from target outcomes using a large secondary dataset (e.g., Gartner/EY supply chain surveys). Second, we will validate and refine these configurations via fuzzy-set Qualitative Comparative Analysis (fsQCA) on a purpose-built international survey of 300+ firms, identifying necessary and sufficient causal pathways linking practice bundles to outcomes.
proposals.contribution: This research will provide a novel decision-support framework for managers to back-cast required actions from sustainability goals. It will theoretically advance the integration of lean and green paradigms by modeling their synergistic and trade-off effects as an inverse design problem, moving beyond correlational studies.
proposals.novelty: While forward models of practice-performance links are common, the explicit formulation and solution of the *inverse* sustainability design problem in supply chains is novel. The integration of inverse optimization with fsQCA to bridge quantitative prescription and qualitative configurational validation represents a significant methodological advance.

proposals.title: Quantifying Supply Chain Resilience: An Ecological Analogy for Structural Risk Assessment
proposals.motivation: Global supply chains face escalating risks from climate disruptions, geopolitical tensions, and pandemics, yet existing risk assessment frameworks often lack a holistic, structural, and predictive measure of systemic resilience. Pre-2026 literature in sustainable supply chain management calls for more robust, quantifiable metrics that can integrate environmental and operational factors to evaluate adaptive capacity before a disruption occurs.
proposals.hypothesis: A composite resilience index, derived by formally mapping key ecological resilience principles (biodiversity, redundancy, connectivity) to supply chain structural properties, will provide a valid and predictive measure of a supply chain's risk exposure and adaptive capacity to systemic shocks.
proposals.method: The research will employ a three-stage mixed-methods approach. First, a formal mapping will be established using expert Delphi panels to link ecological metrics (e.g., Shannon Diversity Index, modularity) to supply chain analogs (supplier base diversity, backup capacity, network path multiplicity). Second, a calculable Supply Chain Resilience Index (SCRI) will be constructed using multi-criteria decision analysis (MCDA) techniques, specifically the Analytical Hierarchy Process (AHP) for weight assignment. Third, the index's predictive validity will be empirically tested via structural equation modeling (SEM) on survey data from international firms, correlating SCRI scores with historical disruption recovery performance.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide a novel, theory-grounded, and calculable metric for supply chain resilience that directly integrates sustainability principles into risk management. It will advance the field of sustainable logistics by offering a standardized tool for firms and policymakers to benchmark and improve the structural robustness of global trade networks against environmental and operational shocks.
proposals.novelty: While analogies between ecosystems and supply chains are noted in pre-2026 literature, this research provides a formal, quantitative translation of established ecological resilience indices into a concrete supply chain assessment tool, moving beyond metaphor to a testable, integrated measurement model.

proposals.title: A Multi-Factor Supply Chain Sustainability Evaluation Framework with Integrated Operational and Disruption Risk Assessment
proposals.motivation: Pre-2026, global supply chains face escalating pressures from climate change, geopolitical tensions, and pandemics, which threaten both environmental and operational performance. Existing sustainability evaluation frameworks, such as emergy analysis and Logistics 4.0 metrics, often treat risk factors as peripheral, creating a critical gap in assessing the true resilience and long-term viability of sustainable supply chain designs. A unified framework is needed to help managers and policymakers make decisions that are simultaneously green and robust.
proposals.hypothesis: Explicitly integrating quantitative supply chain risk metrics into multi-factor sustainability evaluations will reveal significant trade-offs and synergies, leading to the identification of supply chain configurations that are both more sustainable and more resilient than those optimized for sustainability alone.
proposals.method: The research will develop a hybrid analytical model. First, it will extend established multi-criteria decision analysis (MCDA) frameworks, like the Analytical Hierarchy Process (AHP), to include risk dimensions (e.g., supplier concentration risk, geopolitical exposure, natural hazard vulnerability) alongside traditional environmental (e.g., emergy, carbon footprint) and operational (e.g., Logistics 4.0 maturity) factors. Second, it will employ structural equation modeling (SEM) on survey data from international firms to validate the proposed interrelationships between risk, sustainability, and performance constructs. The model will be tested using case data from Belt and Road Initiative and ASEAN trade corridors.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide a validated, integrated decision-support tool for sustainable supply chain management that explicitly accounts for resilience. It will advance the field of supply chain environmental accounting by demonstrating how risk exposure modulates the effectiveness of sustainability initiatives. The findings will offer actionable insights for firms navigating the complex trade-offs inherent in greening global logistics networks.
proposals.novelty: While risk and sustainability are both well-studied, their formal integration within a single, empirically validated multi-factor evaluation framework for global supply chains is novel. This moves beyond treating risk as a separate silo, instead modeling it as a core dimension that interacts dynamically with environmental and operational performance metrics.

proposals.title: Simulating the Socio-Ecological Metabolism of Global Supply Networks: An Agent-Based Model Integrating Dynamic Material Flow and Life Cycle Assessment
proposals.motivation: Global supply networks are complex adaptive systems where decentralized decisions by firms drive aggregate environmental impacts like carbon emissions and resource depletion. Static industrial ecology tools (MFA, LCA) and linear supply chain models fail to capture how policies, disruptions, and technological innovations dynamically propagate through these networks, limiting effective sustainability governance. This gap is critical in the context of global initiatives like the Belt and Road and the push for Logistics 4.0, which require understanding systemic, emergent environmental consequences.
proposals.hypothesis: The integration of agent-based decision-making with dynamic, agent-specific MFA and LCA data will reveal non-linear pathways and tipping points in a supply network's environmental metabolism, demonstrating that network-level resource efficiency and resilience emerge from localized agent interactions constrained by real-time environmental performance feedback.
proposals.method: We will develop an ABM platform in NetLogo or AnyLogic where autonomous buyer and supplier agents, parameterized from international industry survey data, make sourcing and production decisions based on multi-criteria rules (cost, capacity, environmental score). Each agent will be linked to a dynamic material flow account and a simplified, modular LCA engine (using pre-2026 databases like ecoinvent) that updates environmental impacts (carbon, water, waste) in real-time per transaction. The simulation will test scenarios (carbon tariffs, resource shocks) using Monte Carlo methods to analyze emergent network structures and metabolic profiles.
proposals.contribution: This work contributes a novel computational laboratory for testing sustainable supply chain policies and circular economy strategies before real-world implementation. It advances the field of supply chain environmental accounting by moving from static, retrospective analysis to dynamic, prospective simulation of socio-ecological systems, directly supporting the 'greening' of global trade frameworks.
proposals.novelty: While ABM and industrial ecology are established, their fusion into a dynamic 'metabolism' simulation where every agent carries and updates its own MFA/LCA profile based on decentralized decisions is a significant methodological leap beyond current static hybrid LCA-IO or descriptive network models.

proposals.title: A Lean Multi-Criteria Framework for Sustainable Supplier Selection in SME Supply Chains: Integrating Lightweight Data Analytics and Managerial Heuristics
proposals.motivation: Small and medium-sized enterprises (SMEs) are critical links in global supply chains but lack the resources to implement comprehensive, data-intensive sustainable supplier selection frameworks designed for large corporations. This gap hinders the diffusion of sustainability practices and creates blind spots in supply chain environmental accounting, especially within networks like the Belt and Road Initiative where SMEs are prevalent. A scalable, pragmatic solution is needed to enable SMEs to make informed, greener procurement decisions.
proposals.hypothesis: A simplified, hybrid decision-support framework that combines minimal viable environmental data points with structured managerial judgment can yield supplier selection outcomes for SMEs that are both pragmatically feasible and significantly improve upon conventional cost-only selection.
proposals.method: The research will employ a design science approach to develop and test a lean multi-criteria decision-making (MCDM) model. It will adapt and scale down established methods like the Analytical Hierarchy Process (AHP) by reducing criteria complexity and integrating lightweight data proxies (e.g., simplified emergy indicators, regional environmental performance indices). The framework's usability and effectiveness will be empirically validated through a structured international survey of SME managers, akin to the Logistics 4.0 validation method, followed by case study application in selected SME supply chains.
proposals.contribution: This work will contribute a theoretically grounded yet practitioner-accessible tool for sustainable SME procurement, directly addressing the 'scale down' challenge. It will advance the field of supply chain environmental accounting by proposing standardized, minimal data requirements for SME-level assessments, thereby supporting broader supply chain transparency and the greening of trade networks involving smaller firms.
proposals.novelty: The novelty lies in the systematic adaptation of comprehensive sustainability assessment frameworks for the severe resource constraints of SMEs, moving beyond mere checklist simplification to a hybrid model that formally integrates sparse objective data with calibrated managerial heuristics, a gap not sufficiently addressed in pre-2026 literature.

proposals.title: A Multi-Tier, Multi-Objective Optimization Model for Sustainable Supplier Selection in Global Supply Networks
proposals.motivation: Current sustainable supplier selection models predominantly focus on first-tier partners, neglecting the significant environmental and social impacts embedded in sub-tier supply networks. This oversight is critical as global initiatives like the Belt and Road and pressures for Scope 3 emissions accounting demand holistic supply chain transparency. A scalable method for multi-tier selection is essential for genuine supply chain greening and robust global environmental accounting.
proposals.hypothesis: A multi-objective optimization model that simultaneously evaluates primary and sub-tier suppliers can identify network configurations that yield superior overall sustainability performance compared to sequential or tier-by-tier selection approaches. The integration of embodied resource flows (e.g., emergy) and digital traceability (e.g., blockchain attributes) will provide a more accurate and actionable assessment.
proposals.method: We will develop a bi-level or multi-objective mixed-integer programming model that minimizes total cost and total embodied environmental footprint (using emergy evaluation as a comprehensive metric) across a three-tier network. The model will incorporate constraints for capacity, risk, and compliance, drawing on Logistics 4.0 survey data for parameterization. Solution approaches will employ pre-2026 metaheuristics (e.g., NSGA-II) to handle the computational complexity of large-scale networks. The model will be empirically tested using a case study of a firm's trade network with ASEAN countries.
proposals.contribution: This research contributes a novel, integrated decision-support framework for sustainable multi-tier supply chain design, advancing the field beyond first-tier focus. It provides a methodological bridge between macro-level embodied resource accounting (e.g., emergy evaluation of trade) and micro-level operational supplier selection, enhancing the practical implementation of global supply chain environmental accounting.
proposals.novelty: The novelty lies in the simultaneous, network-optimized selection of primary and sub-tier suppliers using a hybrid model that combines emergy evaluation for environmental assessment with optimization techniques tailored for multi-echelon structures, a significant extension beyond the current sequential or dyadic supplier evaluation models.

proposals.title: A Multi-Level Perspective Framework for Dynamic Supplier Development in Sustainable Supply Chain Transitions
proposals.motivation: Current supplier sustainability assessments are static and fail to account for the dynamic, systemic nature of socio-technical transitions, such as the shift to circular economies or low-carbon logistics. This leaves suppliers and focal firms reactive, investing in capabilities that may become obsolete or misaligned with emerging landscape pressures and niche innovations. A method to generate forward-looking, context-specific improvement pathways is critically needed for strategic supply chain greening.
proposals.hypothesis: Integrating the Multi-Level Perspective (MLP) with inverse optimization techniques can generate more effective, transition-aware supplier development guidance than static benchmarking models. The guidance will vary significantly based on a supplier's position in the regime and the anticipated evolution of niche, regime, and landscape factors.
proposals.method: We will develop a three-phase computational framework. First, we will map the transition landscape using text mining of policy, patent, and sustainability report databases to identify and weight niche innovations, regime rules, and landscape trends. Second, we will model the transition as a system of influence pathways between MLP levels. Third, we will apply inverse optimization: given a desired future state (e.g., a 'tipping point' in renewable energy adoption), the model will solve backwards to identify the minimal set of capability investments (e.g., ISO 50001, modular design) a specific supplier must make, and their optimal timing.
proposals.contribution: This research contributes a novel, dynamic theory of supplier development grounded in socio-technical transition theory. It provides supply chain managers with a decision-support tool for strategic supplier investment and offers suppliers a contextual roadmap to navigate systemic change proactively, moving beyond compliance to catalytic action.
proposals.novelty: The approach is novel in its fusion of the MLP—a macro-scale transition theory—with the micro-scale, prescriptive logic of inverse optimization for supplier management. It moves from diagnosing a system's state to generating targeted, time-phased action plans for individual actors within it.

proposals.title: Modeling the Diffusion of Blockchain for Environmental Accounting in Global Supply Chains: An Agent-Based Simulation of Stakeholder Adoption Dynamics
proposals.motivation: The integration of blockchain technology for embodied environmental resource accounting, as proposed in recent literature, promises to enhance transparency and sustainability in global trade. However, its adoption is hindered by complex multi-stakeholder dynamics involving varying incentives, power asymmetries, and risk perceptions among suppliers, manufacturers, logistics firms, and regulators. Understanding these non-linear adoption pathways is critical for designing effective policies and business strategies to accelerate green supply chain initiatives.
proposals.hypothesis: The adoption of blockchain-enabled environmental accounting in a supply network follows a threshold-driven, non-linear path, where early adoption by dominant focal firms and regulatory pressure can trigger cascading adoption among smaller suppliers, but is significantly moderated by perceived cost-benefit ratios and network topology.
proposals.method: We will develop an agent-based model (ABM) using a modified version of the NetLogo platform, incorporating agents representing firms across a multi-tier supply chain. Agent rules will be informed by the Theory of Planned Behavior and diffusion of innovation theory. The simulation will implement a threshold adoption algorithm, where an agent adopts blockchain based on a weighted function of peer pressure (network connections), economic incentives (costs vs. green premium), and coercive pressure (regulatory mandates). We will calibrate initial parameters using data from prior surveys on Logistics 4.0 and case studies of Belt and Road Initiative trade, then run Monte Carlo simulations to explore adoption trajectories under different scenarios.
proposals.contribution: This research will contribute a dynamic, systems-level understanding of technology adoption for sustainability, moving beyond static case studies. It will provide supply chain managers and policymakers with a simulation tool to test intervention strategies (e.g., subsidies, mandates) for promoting blockchain-based environmental accounting. The work will also extend agent-based modeling applications into the nascent field of green digital infrastructure.
proposals.novelty: While ABM is used in supply chain management and blockchain research separately, its application to model the adoption dynamics of blockchain specifically for embodied environmental resource accounting is novel. This approach uniquely captures the interplay between technological innovation, sustainability incentives, and the complex network structure of global supply chains, a gap in the current literature.

proposals.title: Assessing Blockchain for Green Supply Chains: An Empirical Test of the DeLone & McLean IS Success Model
proposals.motivation: Blockchain is widely proposed as a transformative technology for enhancing transparency and sustainability in global supply chains. However, its actual success and impact remain anecdotal, lacking rigorous evaluation against established theoretical frameworks. Grounding assessment in pre-2026 Information Systems (IS) success models is critical to move beyond hype and provide actionable insights for managers and policymakers investing in these systems.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that the quality dimensions (system, information, service) of a blockchain-based supply chain information system, as defined by the updated DeLone & McLean IS Success Model, will have a significant positive effect on user satisfaction and net benefits, which in turn will positively influence the environmental performance (e.g., reduced waste, improved resource tracking) of the green supply chain.
proposals.method: The research will employ a mixed-methods, survey-based approach. First, we will develop and validate a structural equation model (SEM) based on the DeLone & McLean framework, adapting constructs to the context of blockchain for green supply chains (e.g., 'information quality' includes embodied carbon data accuracy). The survey instrument will be distributed to a global sample of supply chain and sustainability professionals in firms piloting or using blockchain. Partial Least Squares SEM (PLS-SEM) will be the primary algorithm for testing the hypothesized relationships and model fit.
proposals.contribution: This study will provide one of the first theoretically grounded, empirical validations of blockchain's success in sustainable supply chain management. It will contribute a validated measurement model for Logistics/Supply Chain 4.0 technologies and offer evidence-based guidance on which blockchain system qualities most directly drive environmental performance improvements.
proposals.novelty: While IS success models are well-established, their application to evaluate a nascent, sustainability-focused technology like blockchain in the complex, multi-stakeholder context of global green supply chains is novel. This bridges a critical gap between information systems theory and sustainable supply chain management practice.

proposals.title: A Blockchain-Enabled Embodied Resource Accounting System (BERAS) for Global Supply Chain Circularity
proposals.motivation: Current global supply chains lack standardized, verifiable data on resource extraction, use, and recycling, hindering effective circular economy transitions and environmental accounting. This opacity, as highlighted in pre-2026 work on greening initiatives like the BRI, prevents accurate assessment of embodied environmental resources and creates barriers to sustainable logistics and trade policies. A unified global data system is critical for moving beyond firm-level reporting to a systemic, chain-wide perspective.
proposals.hypothesis: A distributed, blockchain-anchored data architecture, integrating multi-tier supply chain data with national material flow accounts, can provide a verifiable and scalable foundation for global resource use monitoring and circularity performance assessment.
proposals.method: The method builds on pre-2026 blockchain designs for environmental accounting by proposing a hybrid system architecture. A permissioned blockchain (e.g., Hyperledger Fabric) will serve as an immutable ledger for transactions of materials and recycled content, using smart contracts to automate reporting against standards like the EU's Circular Economy Monitoring Framework. Data ingestion will utilize IoT sensors for logistics 4.0 and API integrations with existing platforms (e.g., national trade databases, industry digital twins). A semantic layer will align disparate data using an ontology based on the UNEP International Resource Panel's material flow categories, enabling embodied resource calculation via input-output and emergy analysis algorithms.
proposals.contribution: This research will contribute a novel, integrated technical prototype for global resource data aggregation, advancing the field of supply chain environmental accounting from conceptual models to a functional system. It will provide empirical insights into data governance and interoperability challenges, offering policymakers and industry a blueprint for transparent, circular supply chain management.
proposals.novelty: The novelty lies in the specific integration of a permissioned blockchain for data integrity with a semantic ontology for global data harmonization, directly linking real-time logistics 4.0 data with macro-level embodied resource evaluation frameworks like emergy analysis, which has not been fully operationalized at a global scale.

proposals.title: A Blockchain-Enabled Traceability Framework for Global Material Flows and Circular Economy Performance
proposals.motivation: Global material flows, particularly for critical secondary resources, suffer from opaque and fragmented tracking, hindering effective recycling and circular economy transitions. Pre-2026, the Belt and Road Initiative and other global trade networks have amplified these flows without robust environmental accounting. This opacity impedes policy-making, corporate responsibility, and the verification of sustainable supply chain claims.
proposals.hypothesis: Implementing a hybrid blockchain-RFID traceability system for specific global material flows (e.g., post-consumer plastics or e-waste) will significantly improve the accuracy of embodied environmental resource accounting and reveal previously hidden inefficiencies in recycling networks.
proposals.method: The research will design and simulate a permissioned blockchain architecture (e.g., Hyperledger Fabric) integrated with RFID data capture points at key nodes (collection, sorting, processing, re-manufacturing). Smart contracts will automate the recording of material quantity, quality, and embodied emergy (based on pre-2026 emergy evaluation methods) at each transfer. The system will be tested via an agent-based model simulating trade flows between China and ASEAN, using empirical data from prior surveys to validate the model's ability to track material loss and recovery rates.
proposals.contribution: This work will contribute a novel, operational framework for global material flow accounting that bridges supply chain traceability tech with environmental management. It will provide empirical evidence on the potential of digital twins for circular economy performance measurement and offer actionable insights for greening initiatives like the BRI.
proposals.novelty: While blockchain has been proposed for supply chain provenance, its integration with embodied emergy accounting for specific, hard-to-track global recycling flows is novel. This moves beyond product-centric tracking to an aggregated, environmental-resource-focused system for secondary materials.

proposals.title: Beyond Strategic Intent: A Multi-Method Framework for Measuring the Tangible Environmental and Social Outcomes of Sustainable Supply Chain Initiatives
proposals.motivation: Current sustainability assessments in global supply chains often conflate strategic intent and communication with actual performance, leading to greenwashing and misallocated resources. This gap is critical as international trade, logistics, and initiatives like the Belt and Road expand, increasing their environmental and social footprint. A rigorous, outcome-based metric is needed to move from symbolic to substantive sustainability.
proposals.hypothesis: The strategic embedding of sustainability in supply chain governance is a necessary but insufficient condition for success; the primary success metric is the measurable, tangible improvement in environmental resource efficiency and social equity outcomes at operational nodes and across the network.
proposals.method: The research will employ a multi-method, multi-tiered approach. First, a large-scale international survey of supply chain managers (similar to Logistics 4.0 validation studies) will capture data on strategic practices and perceived outcomes. Second, this will be triangulated with quantitative trade and environmental data (e.g., embodied energy, water, emissions) using Input-Output analysis and emergy evaluation (as in the ASEAN trade paper) to calculate tangible environmental outcomes. Third, a proof-of-concept blockchain architecture (inspired by the embodied resources blockchain design) will be modeled to trace and verify specific social outcome claims (e.g., fair wage payments, community investment) against strategic commitments.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide a validated, operationalizable framework for distinguishing between strategic symbolism and substantive sustainability performance in global supply chains. It will contribute to the fields of sustainable logistics and supply chain management by offering a robust, multi-dimensional measurement model grounded in tangible outcomes, advancing environmental accounting and governance.
proposals.novelty: The novelty lies in the systematic integration of perceptual survey data, physical environmental accounting (emergy), and traceability technology to create a composite metric that explicitly prioritizes tangible outcomes over strategic intent, a direct empirical test of a core assumption in the field.

proposals.title: From Dialogue to Decision: A Multi-Method Analysis of Stakeholder Influence on Corporate Sustainability Prioritization in Global Supply Chains
proposals.motivation: Despite widespread corporate adoption of stakeholder engagement for sustainability, there is limited empirical evidence on how this dialogue concretely shapes which environmental and social issues firms prioritize and act upon within their supply chains. This gap is critical as global initiatives like the Belt and Road and evolving trade policies increase scrutiny on supply chain impacts. Understanding the translation of dialogue into strategic priorities is essential for effective sustainability governance.
proposals.hypothesis: The intensity and structure of stakeholder dialogue, particularly with supply chain partners and industry consortia, significantly and measurably influence the ranking and resource allocation for specific sustainability issues (e.g., carbon, water, labor) within a firm's supply chain management strategy, beyond the influence of regulatory pressure alone.
proposals.method: The study will employ a sequential mixed-methods design. First, a quantitative analysis using structural equation modeling (SEM) on survey data from international supply chain managers will test relationships between stakeholder engagement types (e.g., frequency, formality) and reported sustainability priority shifts. Second, qualitative comparative case studies of firms in high-trade industries will use content analysis of sustainability reports and interview transcripts, applying natural language processing (NLP) techniques like topic modeling (Latent Dirichlet Allocation) to trace the evolution of issue salience in corporate communications relative to documented stakeholder dialogues.
proposals.contribution: This research will provide a validated framework for mapping stakeholder influence onto corporate sustainability agendas, contributing to stakeholder theory and sustainable supply chain management literature. It will offer practical insights for managers on designing engagement mechanisms that effectively translate external concerns into actionable supply chain priorities.
proposals.novelty: It moves beyond assessing engagement *presence* to empirically measuring its *impact* on specific priority-setting, integrating SEM with NLP-based text analysis to link dialogue content with strategic outcomes in a supply chain context.

proposals.title: Mapping the Sustainability Discourse in SME Supply Chains: A Cross-Industry Content Analysis
proposals.motivation: Small and medium enterprises (SMEs) constitute the majority of global businesses and are deeply embedded in global supply chains, yet their specific sustainability challenges and strategies are under-researched compared to large multinationals. Understanding these challenges is critical, as SMEs often lack the resources for formal sustainability reporting and face unique barriers in greening their operations and logistics. A systematic analysis of how SMEs frame and address sustainability is needed to inform targeted policy and support mechanisms.
proposals.hypothesis: The sustainability discourse within SME supply chains differs significantly from that of large corporations, being more focused on operational efficiency, regulatory compliance, and localized resource constraints rather than strategic global environmental accounting. Furthermore, the adoption of digital technologies (Logistics 4.0) for sustainability in SMEs is primarily driven by cost and customer pressure rather than internal environmental strategy.
proposals.method: We will apply a structured content analysis methodology, similar to that used in Sarkis's prior work on global supply chain environmental accounting, to a large corpus of SME-focused sustainability reports, industry association publications, and trade media from 2020-2025. Using natural language processing (NLP) techniques, specifically Latent Dirichlet Allocation (LDA) for topic modeling and sentiment analysis via pre-2026 libraries like NLTK or spaCy, we will code and quantify key themes. The analysis will categorize mentions of environmental resources, logistics technologies, trade dependencies, and management practices, comparing findings across industries and regions.
proposals.contribution: This study will provide a granular, evidence-based map of sustainability priorities and implementation gaps within SME supply chains, contributing to the literature on sustainable supply chain management at the SME level. It will offer practical insights for developing more accessible sustainability tools and policies tailored to SME capacities and constraints, thereby supporting broader supply chain decarbonization goals.
proposals.novelty: While content analysis is established, its application is novel here by shifting the focus from large corporations and mega-projects (like the Belt and Road Initiative) to the heterogeneous SME sector, and by integrating analysis of digital logistics adoption with traditional environmental resource concerns specific to smaller-scale operations.

proposals.title: Modeling the Adoption of Collaborative Sustainability Tools in Global Supply Chains: A Behavioral Economics and Agent-Based Simulation Approach
proposals.motivation: Despite the proliferation of digital tools like blockchain for supply chain transparency and Logistics 4.0 technologies, their adoption for collaborative sustainability goals remains slow and fragmented. This gap persists because traditional adoption models focus on technical and economic factors, neglecting the cognitive biases, social preferences, and bounded rationality of supply chain decision-makers. Addressing this behavioral gap is critical for accelerating the greening of global trade networks, such as the Belt and Road Initiative.
proposals.hypothesis: The adoption of collaborative sustainability tools is primarily driven by a combination of behavioral factors—including loss aversion, social proof, and present bias—interacting with traditional economic incentives, and this interaction can be formally modeled to predict adoption patterns across heterogeneous supply chain networks.
proposals.method: We will develop an agent-based simulation (ABS) model where agents represent firms in a multi-tier supply chain. Agent decision rules will be informed by a structural equation model (SEM) derived from a global survey of supply chain managers, measuring constructs from behavioral economics (e.g., prospect theory's value function, hyperbolic discounting). The simulation will integrate these behavioral parameters with traditional variables (cost, regulatory pressure) to model the diffusion of a tool like an embodied environmental resources blockchain. Validation will use empirical data from prior studies on trade with ASEAN and Logistics 4.0 adoption.
proposals.contribution: This research will contribute a novel, behaviorally-grounded simulation framework for predicting technology adoption in sustainable supply chains, moving beyond descriptive theories. It will provide actionable insights for policymakers and technology designers on which behavioral 'nudges' or incentive structures are most effective for fostering collaboration across complex, global networks.
proposals.novelty: While behavioral economics has been applied to consumer green choices, its integration with agent-based modeling to explain firm-level adoption of inter-organizational sustainability tools in supply chains is novel. This bridges the micro-level behavioral theory with the macro-level system dynamics of global trade and environmental accounting.

proposals.title: Scalability of Embodied Environmental Resource Accounting in Global Multi-Tier Supply Networks: A Hybrid Blockchain-Graph Database Simulation
proposals.motivation: Pre-2026, global supply chains are increasingly complex, multi-tiered networks, making comprehensive environmental accounting and greening initiatives like the Belt and Road extremely challenging. Current Life Cycle Assessment (LCA) and blockchain-based accounting prototypes are tested on simplified, dyadic relationships, failing to capture the data volume, heterogeneity, and dynamic interactions of real-world multi-tier systems. This gap limits the practical implementation of global supply chain environmental management and policy.
proposals.hypothesis: A hybrid architectural system combining a permissioned blockchain for core transaction integrity with a graph database for complex network analytics can scale effectively to provide verifiable, near-real-time embodied environmental resource accounting across global multi-tier supply networks.
proposals.method: We will develop an agent-based simulation model representing a multi-tier network (e.g., electronics or automotive) with 500+ node agents across 3+ geographic regions. Each agent runs a lightweight client of a permissioned blockchain (Hyperledger Fabric architecture) to record material and embodied resource (e.g., emergy, carbon) transactions. A graph database (Neo4j) will be interfaced to store and continuously analyze the network topology and flow data from the blockchain ledger. Scalability will be tested by incrementally increasing tiers, transaction frequency, and data payloads, measuring system latency, throughput, and storage requirements against benchmarks.
proposals.contribution: This research will provide a validated, scalable architectural blueprint for multi-tier environmental accounting systems, advancing the field of sustainable supply chain management. It will offer empirical evidence on the technical limits and governance requirements for implementing technologies like blockchain in complex global logistics, directly informing initiatives such as greener trade and Logistics 4.0.
proposals.novelty: While prior work designs blockchain for dyadic trade or surveys Logistics 4.0 adoption, this proposal uniquely tests the integration of blockchain with graph analytics for *scalable* environmental accounting across *simulated global multi-tier networks*, a critical step towards practical application.

proposals.title: Optimizing Inter-Organizational Collaboration for Minimum Viable Sustainability in Global Supply Chains
proposals.motivation: Achieving sustainability targets in complex global supply chains often requires extensive collaboration, which is costly and difficult to coordinate. However, the precise level of collaboration needed to meet specific environmental performance thresholds remains poorly understood, leading to inefficiencies and suboptimal resource allocation. This research addresses a critical gap in sustainable supply chain management by determining the minimal collaborative effort required for compliance and performance.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that for a given set of sustainability performance thresholds (e.g., carbon footprint, resource efficiency), there exists a non-linear, quantifiable relationship with the level of inter-organizational collaboration, and that an optimal, minimal collaboration configuration can be algorithmically identified.
proposals.method: The study will employ a multi-method approach. First, we will develop a multi-objective optimization model integrating agent-based simulation to represent dyadic and network-level collaboration (e.g., information sharing, joint planning). The model will be parameterized using empirical data from an international survey of firms engaged in green logistics (extending the Logistics 4.0 measurement model). Specific sustainability thresholds will be defined using embodied environmental resource accounting (e.g., emergy, as used in Belt and Road research) and blockchain-verified data concepts. Algorithms such as NSGA-II (Non-dominated Sorting Genetic Algorithm II) will be used to solve the dual-objective problem of minimizing collaboration intensity while meeting sustainability constraints.
proposals.contribution: This work will contribute a novel decision-support framework for managers to achieve sustainability goals with minimal relational and transactional costs. It will advance theory by formally modeling the collaboration-sustainability trade-off, providing empirical evidence of its shape and boundaries. The findings will offer practical guidance for structuring lean, effective green supply chain partnerships.
proposals.novelty: While prior research establishes that collaboration improves sustainability, this study uniquely inverts the problem to find the *minimum sufficient* collaboration, a cost-efficiency perspective rarely taken. It innovatively combines multi-objective optimization with embodied environmental accounting and survey-based behavioral data to solve this dual problem.

ideas.title: Robust Sustainability Transitions in POM Under Deep Value-Impact Ambiguity
ideas.description: This research direction challenges the assumption of perfect, stable information in sustainable operations management. It investigates how firms can design production networks, circular systems, and green technologies when both the quantitative environmental impacts (e.g., true carbon footprint of a novel material) and the normative weights of sustainability criteria (e.g., societal trade-offs between carbon, biodiversity, and equity) are deeply ambiguous and subject to change. The core contribution is developing novel POM decision models and heuristics that identify strategies robust across a wide range of plausible future valuations and impact assessments, rather than optimal for a single forecast. This enables the field to guide meaningful action toward sustainability despite fundamental scientific and ethical uncertainties, preventing paralysis or commitment to fragile solutions.

ideas.title: Axiomatic Stress-Testing: Identifying and Modeling the Contradiction Points Where Sustainability Constraints Force New Production Theory
ideas.description: This research moves beyond a broad investigation to systematically stress-test the foundational axioms of Production and Operations Management (POM) theory against non-negotiable sustainability constraints. It begins by mapping specific, high-stakes contradiction points, such as the conflict between lean system efficiency and climate-driven supply chain resilience. Using a combination of computational agent-based modeling, comparative historical analysis of sectors undergoing green transitions, and formal conceptual analysis, the project models the breaking points of traditional theory. The outcome is a set of conditioned, falsifiable propositions for new or adapted POM theoretical constructs that are robust to sustainability imperatives, providing a concrete basis for future empirical validation and theoretical development.

ideas.title: Transition-Activated Supplier Guidance: Using Multi-Level Perspective to Generate Targeted Improvement Pathways
ideas.description: This research integrates socio-technical transition theory, specifically the Multi-Level Perspective (MLP), with inverse problem-solving for supplier development. Instead of using static model criteria, it analyzes the evolving landscape (niche innovations, regime pressures, landscape trends) of a sustainable production transition. The system then generates contextualized improvement guidance for suppliers, identifying which specific capabilities (e.g., circular design, renewable energy integration) to develop and when, based on their position within the transitioning system and anticipated tipping points. This provides a strategic roadmap for suppliers to not just improve, but to actively catalyze and navigate the broader systemic shift.

ideas.title: Triangulated Dynamic Network Analysis for Auditing Supply Chain Sustainability Authenticity and Resilience
ideas.description: This research designs a system that moves beyond descriptive mapping to audit the authenticity and resilience of sustainability in supply networks. It integrates traditional SNA with forensic data triangulation, using independent environmental, social, and regulatory data to validate declared practices and identify strategic misrepresentation. The core innovation is a dynamic, agent-based simulation model that uses this validated network to stress-test how sustainability claims and actual performance evolve under external pressures, revealing which network structures and actors genuinely drive impact versus those merely performing influence. The outcome is a diagnostic tool for policymakers and lead firms to distinguish true sustainability leaders from influencers and to fortify networks against systemic greenwashing risks.

ideas.title: Agent-Based Industrial Ecology for Dynamic Supply Network Metabolism
ideas.description: This research direction integrates agent-based modeling (ABM) with industrial ecology's material flow analysis (MFA) and life cycle assessment (LCA) to create a dynamic simulation platform for sustainable supply networks. Autonomous buyer and supplier agents make decisions based on cost, capacity, and, crucially, real-time environmental performance data derived from LCA databases and MFA. The model simulates how these decentralized interactions lead to the emergent evolution of the network's overall resource efficiency, waste streams, and carbon footprint, allowing researchers to test how policies, disruptions, or new technologies propagate through the system's socio-ecological metabolism.

ideas.title: Navigating High-Risk, High-Reward Pathways for Resilient Production Systems
ideas.description: This research investigates difficult and complex production pathways—such as hyper-localized bio-factories, fully circular material flows, or decentralized manufacturing networks—that are often avoided due to perceived technical and economic risks. It formally integrates dynamic, multi-tier supply chain risk metrics (e.g., geopolitical fragility, resource criticality, network complexity) into the evaluation framework to quantify the resilience payoff of these challenging alternatives. The novel synergy lies in using risk exposure not merely as a filter to reject options, but as a diagnostic tool to identify which 'high-risk' production paradigms actually offer the greatest potential to reconfigure and future-proof supply chains against systemic shocks. The outcome is a decision-support model that guides investment toward the most strategically resilient, rather than merely the most immediately viable, sustainable production transformations.

ideas.title: Historical Text Mining for the Evolution of Corporate Environmental Accountability
ideas.description: This research employs advanced NLP text mining on a large-scale historical corpus of news articles, financial reports, and trade publications from the 20th and early 21st centuries to extract and score intangible factors related to environmental responsibility in supply chains and production. By analyzing this longitudinal data, it challenges the ahistorical narrative of sustainability as a purely modern concern, instead mapping its evolving presence, framing, and perceived materiality over time. The methodology enables the identification of historical periods of intense environmental scrutiny, forgotten best practices, and the factors that caused sustainability to recede from or re-enter corporate discourse, providing a data-driven foundation for understanding its long-term cyclical nature.

ideas.title: Network Orchestration for Sustainability: Assigning Agency and Accountability in Inter-firm Ecosystems
ideas.description: This research program moves beyond the firm not just descriptively but prescriptively, by developing a method to assign and operationalize sustainability responsibility within networks. It integrates structural analysis of value chains or ecosystems with a theory of orchestration, pinpointing which actors have the capability and position to steer collective outcomes. The output is a diagnostic and governance toolkit that defines clear roles, leverage points, and accountability mechanisms for key network actors, transforming diffuse networks into actionable governance structures.

ideas.title: Strategic Game-Theoretic Modeling of Multi-Actor Dynamics in Socio-Technical Transitions to Sustainable Production
ideas.description: This research integrates socio-technical transition theory with non-cooperative and cooperative game theory to model the strategic landscape of shifting to sustainable production systems. It conceptualizes the transition as a dynamic, multi-level game where buyers, incumbent suppliers, green technology entrants, and other regime actors engage in negotiations, form coalitions, and make strategic investments under uncertainty. The framework allows for the analysis of how different policy instruments, certification schemes, or network structures alter the payoff matrices and equilibrium outcomes, identifying leverage points to overcome lock-in and orchestrate a coordinated shift. This provides a predictive and prescriptive tool for designing intervention strategies that align individual economic incentives with the collective goal of a systemic socio-technical transition.

ideas.title: Applying Metabolic Network Analysis to Global Production and Policy
ideas.description: This research adapts the problem-solving framework from systems biology—specifically metabolic control analysis and flux balance analysis—to the context of international trade and sustainable production. In the analogous field, scientists model a cell's metabolism as a network of reactions to find key control points that regulate system-level outcomes. The adapted idea applies this network analysis to global production and policy systems. It involves mapping international supply chains, trade flows, and policy instruments as a complex network, then quantifying the 'flux' of materials, value, and regulatory influence. The goal is to identify which specific nodes (e.g., a particular trade bloc's standards, a critical transit country's regulations) act as the primary control points for scaling sustainable production outcomes across borders. This approach provides a quantitative, systems-level method to predict how policy changes in one part of the network will propagate, enabling the design of more effective and efficient international sustainability governance.

ideas.title: Adapting Epidemiological Network Models to Map and Mitigate Sustainability Risk Propagation in Supply Chains
ideas.description: This research adapts a solution from epidemiology, where social network analysis (SNA) is used to model contact networks for disease transmission, identify superspreader nodes, and design targeted containment strategies. The analogous problem in supply chain sustainability is the opaque propagation of unsustainable practices and risks (e.g., regulatory non-compliance, Scope 3 emissions) through inter-organizational networks. The adapted idea applies epidemiological network models to map supply chains, treating sustainability risks as pathogens. By using SNA to identify central, high-influence 'superspreader' firms and critical risk transmission pathways, the research proposes a method for simulating risk propagation and testing precise, network-targeted intervention strategies—such as focused sustainability audits or collaborative green innovation programs for key nodes—to effectively contain risks and enhance overall network sustainability, moving beyond generic firm-level assessments.

ideas.title: Adapting the Relativity Revolution: How Sustainability Constraints Force a Reformulation of Production Theory
ideas.description: This research seed uses an analogy from theoretical physics to explore how sustainability reshapes production theory. In physics, the introduction of the invariant speed of light as a universal constraint forced a revolutionary reformulation of Newtonian mechanics into Special Relativity, altering fundamental concepts of time, space, and energy. Similarly, we propose to investigate how binding environmental imperatives (e.g., strict carbon budgets, material flow caps) act as a system-wide invariant constraint that forces a fundamental re-examination of core Production and Operations Management (POM) models. The adapted idea is to treat these sustainability constraints not as external add-ons or costs, but as foundational axioms that redefine the operational 'frame of reference.' This research would explore how concepts like economic order quantity, facility location, supply chain coordination, and the very definition of productivity and efficiency must be mathematically and conceptually reformulated when traditional models of unbounded resource consumption and waste assimilation are invalid. The outcome would be a new theoretical framework for POM where optimality is defined relative to a fixed ecological budget, potentially revealing novel, non-linear relationships between input, output, and systemic sustainability.

ideas.title: Mapping Intangible Policy and Reputational Flows in Global Production Networks via NLP
ideas.description: This research employs NLP text mining on global news, corporate reports, and policy documents to construct dynamic, firm-level datasets of intangible factors such as perceived environmental compliance, labor practice controversies, and exposure to trade policy sentiment. These datasets are then integrated into a network analysis of global production chains, enabling the empirical investigation of how intangible reputational and regulatory risks propagate across borders and influence sustainable production outcomes. This approach reveals the non-material architecture of global trade, showing how a scandal or policy shift in one country can reshape production sustainability in distant supplier networks through channels invisible to conventional economic data.

ideas.title: Adapting Ecological Succession Models for Resilient Production Systems
ideas.description: This idea adapts the ecological concept of 'succession' to sustainable production research. In ecology, complex, long-term community evolution is studied by mapping dynamic pathways and key transitional elements rather than aiming for a static optimum. Similarly, this research would explore the 'road less travelled' by investigating production not as a system to be optimized to a fixed point, but as a complex system undergoing a necessary transition. It would map 'production succession' pathways, identifying critical but difficult transitional technologies (e.g., bridging infrastructure), evolving workforce skill sets, and adaptable supply chain configurations that move from linear to circular models. The goal is to develop a framework for building resilient, sustainable production ecosystems by understanding and guiding their natural, complex evolution, embracing the difficulty for greater long-term reward.

ideas.title: Constrained Planetary Systems: An Optimization-First Framework for Sustainable Operations
ideas.description: This research program reorients POM for sustainability by making the explicit modeling of hard environmental constraints and multi-objective trade-offs its core, non-negotiable methodology. It moves beyond broadening horizons to providing a specific analytical framework where operations are designed and analyzed as subsystems of bounded planetary systems (e.g., carbon budgets, watershed capacities, critical material stocks). The output is a suite of new decision models, performance metrics, and strategy formulations that rigorously quantify the operational implications of environmental limits, transforming sustainability from a peripheral concern into a central, defining parameter of operations management theory and practice.

ideas.title: Adapting Ecological Resilience Metrics for Supply Chain Risk Assessment
ideas.description: This idea proposes using an analogy from ecosystem ecology to formally integrate supply chain risk metrics. In ecology, the resilience of an ecosystem to shocks is formally assessed using composite indices that integrate measurable factors like biodiversity, redundancy, and network connectivity. The analogous solution is adapted by mapping these ecological factors to supply chain characteristics—supplier diversity, backup capacity, and logistics network structure—to create a formal, calculable Supply Chain Resilience Index. This index would be explicitly incorporated into the multi-factor evaluation framework, providing a quantifiable, predictive measure of risk exposure and adaptive capacity based on the system's inherent structural properties.

ideas.title: Apply a Planetary Boundaries Framework to Map Production Research Agendas
ideas.description: This idea adapts the solution from Earth System Science's 'Planetary Boundaries' framework to the context of production studies. The analogous problem—how to structure scientific inquiry and policy around critical global environmental limits—was solved by defining quantifiable, interconnected thresholds for key Earth system processes. Adapted here, the research would develop a 'Production Research Boundaries' model, identifying and quantifying critical socio-environmental thresholds for production systems (such as material footprint limits, embedded carbon budgets, and equitable transition metrics). This framework would then be used as a direct, structured mechanism to examine how societal environmental shifts (like adopting circular economy mandates or net-zero targets) influence and must actively reshape production research agendas, prioritizing studies that keep production within these defined safe and just operating spaces.

ideas.title: Adapting the 'Diffusion of Innovations' model from epidemiology to map niche-regime dynamics in sustainable production
ideas.description: This idea adapts the 'Diffusion of Innovations' model from epidemiology and public health to the context of socio-technical transitions in sustainable production. The analogous solution from epidemiology provides a robust, quantitative framework for understanding how innovations spread through a population via specific adopter categories and communication channels. By adapting this solution, the research would apply the diffusion model to map and analyze the systemic shift toward sustainable production, treating novel green technologies or circular economy practices as the 'innovation' to be diffused. This approach would allow for the identification of key leverage points—such as targeting early majority influencers or removing barriers to communication—to accelerate the transition within a specific industrial or socio-technical regime.

ideas.title: Adopting Planetary Boundaries as a Systems Framework for Sustainable POM
ideas.description: This research expands the conceptual scope of POM for sustainability by drawing an analogy from Earth System Science's 'Planetary Boundaries' framework. That framework solved the analogous problem of managing complex, interconnected environmental risks by defining quantifiable, non-negotiable global biophysical thresholds. Adapted to POM, the idea is to develop a methodology for translating these global planetary boundaries (e.g., for carbon emissions, freshwater use, novel entities) into firm- and network-level 'operational boundaries.' This would provide a rigorous, science-based systems framework to guide POM research and practice, moving beyond relative reductions to absolute sustainability, where the core objective is to operate within a defined share of the Earth's safe carrying capacity.

ideas.title: Apply epidemiological contact tracing to industrial symbiosis networks
ideas.description: This idea adapts the problem-solving approach from epidemiology to the context of Production and Operations Management (POM) sustainability. In public health, contact tracing is a core method for modeling the spread of disease through a population to identify critical intervention points. The analogous solution is to apply this network mapping and analysis logic to industrial ecosystems. The adapted research idea is to use a 'contact tracing' methodology to rigorously map material and energy flows between co-located or linked firms in an industrial park or supply chain. By treating resource exchanges like disease transmissions, researchers can identify key 'super-spreader' firms (critical resource hubs), single points of failure, and network pathways for waste or by-products. This allows POM sustainability studies to move beyond static material flow analysis (MFA) of single firms to dynamic, network-based assessments of systemic resilience, risk, and optimization opportunities for circular economy practices.

ideas.title: Comparative causal mapping of systemic lock-in through matched sustainable transitions
ideas.description: This research employs a matched-pair design, selecting cases where unsustainable production persists and pairing them with socio-technically similar systems that have undergone a sustainable transition. Using qualitative comparative analysis (QCA) and process tracing, it dissects the sequences of events, actor coalitions, and institutional changes in both trajectories. This allows for the identification of necessary and sufficient conditions for lock-in versus change, transforming the baseline from a static observation into a dynamic, causal model of systemic barriers.

ideas.title: Computational Transition Modeling for Prescriptive Pathway Design in Sustainable Production
ideas.description: This research integrates socio-technical transition theory with computational simulation and participatory methods to move from descriptive analysis to prescriptive pathway design. It develops formal, data-driven models (e.g., agent-based) that explicitly represent the multi-level interactions between niches, regimes, and landscapes to simulate future transition trajectories. These models are used to stress-test and compare the efficacy of different policy mixes, innovation strategies, and investment priorities. The approach is grounded through iterative engagement with industry, policy, and civil society stakeholders to validate assumptions, co-create scenarios, and identify robust, actionable intervention points for accelerating systemic shifts.

ideas.title: Multi-Tier Supply Chain Selection via Nested Optimization and Hierarchical Coordination
ideas.description: This research expands the original idea of multi-tier supply chain selection by drawing an analogy from computational ecology's solution to nested habitat selection. The analogous problem—where an animal selects a primary home range and then specific foraging patches within it—is solved via hierarchical, staged optimization with constraint propagation. Adapting this, we propose a novel multi-level mathematical programming model for supply networks. The model simultaneously selects primary (Tier-1) and sub-tier (Tier-2, Tier-3) suppliers by structuring the problem into a primary 'territory' selection tier based on strategic, high-level criteria, and nested 'patch' selection tiers for subsequent suppliers based on operational and relational criteria, all coordinated to optimize total network performance, risk, and sustainability. This approach explicitly captures the dependency and constraint relationships between tiers, moving beyond simple sequential or parallel selection models.

ideas.title: Evolutionary Game-Theoretic Framework for Dynamic Supplier Selection and Relationship Management
ideas.description: This research proposes a framework that integrates game theory with longitudinal performance tracking to model supplier relationships as a repeated, evolutionary game. The long-term performance data of selected suppliers is used to empirically validate and dynamically update the payoff structures, strategies, and equilibrium predictions of the initial selection game. This creates a feedback loop where past outcomes inform future negotiation strategies, allowing the model to capture how buyer and supplier behaviors co-evolve over time in response to performance results. Consequently, the framework not only validates initial selection efficacy but also provides an adaptive mechanism for strategic relationship management and re-negotiation throughout the supplier lifecycle.

ideas.title: Adaptive Sampling for SME Supplier Selection: A Heuristic Framework from Ecology
ideas.description: Drawing an analogy from ecology's methods for species monitoring under resource constraints, this research adapts 'adaptive sampling' and 'rapid assessment protocols' to the problem of SME supplier selection. The analogous ecological solution involves using initial, low-effort sampling to guide targeted, in-depth investigation, efficiently managing limited field resources. Adapted to the business context, the research proposes a scalable, dual-stage heuristic framework for SMEs. The framework begins with a rapid initial filter of potential suppliers using a minimal set of key, easily accessible indicators. Suppliers passing this threshold then undergo a second, more tailored evaluation phase that remains proportionate to the SME's data and managerial capacity. This methodologically scales down comprehensive supplier selection models into a practical, adaptive tool suitable for small firms, ensuring rigor is maintained where it counts most without overwhelming limited resources.

ideas.title: Adapting Ecological Niche Construction Theory to Model Supply Network Co-evolution
ideas.description: This research expands the original agent-based modeling idea for supply networks by drawing an analogy from theoretical ecology, specifically niche construction theory. The analogous solution involves simulating how organisms dynamically modify their environment and selection pressures, leading to co-evolution. Adapted to supply networks, the research idea is to model autonomous buyer and supplier agents as active 'niche constructors' whose interactions—such as partnership formation, capability investment, and information sharing—continuously reshape the network's fitness landscape. This creates co-evolutionary feedback loops where micro-level decisions lead to the emergent, dynamic evolution of macro-level network topology, resilience, and performance, moving beyond static selection outcomes to model the ongoing construction of the supply network itself.

ideas.title: A Causal-Mechanistic Framework for Predicting Production Research Agendas Under Environmental Pressure
ideas.description: This research develops and tests a causal-mechanistic model to explain and predict how specific environmental shocks and societal responses (e.g., policy mandates, resource scarcity events, climate disasters) reconfigure production research priorities. It employs a mixed-methods system combining historical analysis of publication and funding data with natural language processing to trace the propagation of environmental signals into research domains. The core innovation is the creation and experimental validation of 'research agenda stress-test' scenarios, allowing stakeholders to proactively evaluate and steer the resilience and relevance of production studies in the face of anticipated socio-environmental evolution.

ideas.title: Adapting Ecological Niche Modeling to Quantify Intangible Supplier Risk
ideas.description: This idea adapts the solution from ecological niche modeling (Species Distribution Modeling) to the financial and supply chain context. In ecology, models predict species presence by learning the correlation between occurrence data and multi-dimensional environmental variables from spatial datasets. Analogously, this research would use NLP text mining on news articles, financial reports, and regulatory filings to extract and quantify intangible 'environmental' variables for suppliers, such as public sentiment, governance controversy, or innovation momentum. These variables become features for a machine learning model trained on historical data of supplier performance or failure. The output is a predictive score for intangible supplier risk factors, framing a supplier's operational 'suitability' much like an organism's ecological niche, predicted from the informational environment it exists within.

ideas.title: Lightweight Portfolio Optimization for SME Supply Base Resilience
ideas.description: This research develops a pragmatic, low-data framework that allows SMEs to construct and manage an optimized portfolio of suppliers, rather than selecting a single source. The framework adapts portfolio optimization principles to the SME context by relying on simplified risk-return metrics, qualitative managerial judgment, and readily available surrogate data. It provides decision-support tools for determining the ideal number of suppliers and allocating order quantities among them to balance cost, reliability, and risk mitigation. The outcome is a democratized approach to strategic sourcing that enhances SME supply chain resilience without requiring sophisticated analytics or large historical datasets.

ideas.title: Applying Historical Ecology's Deep-Time Perspective to Sustainability Research
ideas.description: This research idea inverts the assumption that environmental sustainability in production is a new concern by drawing an analogy from the field of Historical Ecology. Historical ecology solved the analogous problem—the assumption that significant human environmental impact is exclusively modern—by employing interdisciplinary methods to reconstruct long-term socio-ecological systems, uncovering ancient sustainable practices. Adapting this solution, the research would systematically investigate pre-20th century and pre-industrial production systems across different cultures and periods. By analyzing archaeological evidence, historical agricultural and craft treatises, and paleoenvironmental proxies, it aims to build a documented 'deep history' of sustainable resource management. This would challenge the recency narrative in sustainability studies, provide empirically grounded historical analogs for modern challenges, and potentially recover lost or overlooked principles of long-term environmental stewardship embedded in past production regimes.

ideas.title: Stress-Test Framework Generalizability via Dimensional Extremes
ideas.description: This research systematically validates the supplier selection framework's generalizability by applying it to industries chosen not for convenience, but for their theoretical capacity to challenge the model's core assumptions. Industries are selected via a matrix that maps them against extreme values on key framework dimensions, such as high-volatility commodity sourcing versus long-term co-development partnerships. This method ensures the test reveals the framework's true boundaries, potential failure modes, and necessary contextual adaptations, providing a robust, falsifiable assessment of its general applicability.

ideas.title: Text Mining for Micro-Operational Sustainability Intelligence
ideas.description: This research fuses the granularity of micro-operational sustainability with the data extraction power of text mining. It proposes using NLP on internal communications (e.g., work logs, maintenance reports, shift handover notes) and external news to quantify intangible factors—like procedural adherence, safety culture, or resource mindfulness—that drive environmental outcomes at the level of a specific production line or worker team. This creates a dynamic, narrative-based dashboard that moves beyond aggregate facility-level metrics, revealing how daily operational decisions and local narratives correlate with measurable resource efficiency, waste generation, or emission events. The synergy enables a previously impossible, fine-grained diagnosis of the human and procedural drivers of sustainability performance within complex industrial systems.

ideas.title: Micro-Operational Sustainability via Behavioral Nudges and Feedback Loops
ideas.description: This research adapts a solution from behavioral economics and public health, where scaling interventions down to individual behaviors was achieved through nudges and feedback loops. The analogous problem involved changing ingrained daily habits (like handwashing) by making the desired behavior easier and more visible while providing immediate feedback. Adapted to industrial sustainability, the idea is to study environmental impact at the level of individual production lines or worker decisions by instrumenting the micro-operational environment. Sensors would track resource consumption and waste at a granular level, feeding data to interfaces that provide operators with real-time, personalized feedback and behavioral nudges. The research would involve designing, implementing, and testing these socio-technical systems to measure their efficacy in shifting micro-behaviors and aggregating their impact on overall facility sustainability metrics.
